mechanical: Use air-tight storage containers

अनाज की सतह पर रेशमी जाला

diseases.name: coffee rust
symptoms: Yellowish or pale green spots on the upper surface of leaves, which may coalesce into larger lesions in severe cases .
Reddish-brown or orange powdery pustules on the lower surface of leaves, which contain masses of urediniospores .
Premature defoliation of leaves, which reduces photosynthesis and transpiration .
Reduced growth and development of shoots, flowers and fruits .
Lower quality and quantity of coffee beans .

diseases.name: coffee sooty blotch
symptoms: Coffee miner is not a plant disease but a term used to describe a collaborative cryptocurrency mining pool that can be performed in wifi networks using a man-in-the-middle (MITM) attack.
However, some of the disease symptoms of coffee plants are yellowing, browning, or falling of leaves, shriveling, rotting, or mummification of berries, and wilting, dieback, or death of branches.

diseases.name: cotton angular leaf spot
symptoms: On leaves: The disease starts as small, water-soaked lesions (spots) that progress into characteristic angular shapes when the leaf veins restrict the bacterial movement. The lesions appear on the upper side of the leaf, turn black as they expand, and may be surrounded by a yellow ring or halo. The lesions may coalesce and cause defoliation .

On stems: The disease causes black streaks or cankers along the main veins or petioles of infected leaves. The cankers may girdle the stem and cause wilting or death of the plant. The cankers may also crack and ooze bacterial exudate .

On bolls: The disease causes sunken, water-soaked lesions on the boll surface that may turn black or brown. The lesions may penetrate into the boll interior and cause rotting of the lint and seeds. The infected bolls may drop prematurely or remain attached to the plant .

diseases.name: cotton anthracnose
symptoms: Damping-off or seedling blight of seedlings, due to the development of reddish-brown cortical rot at the base of the hypocotyl, and spotting of the cotyledons .

Anthracnose lesions on all plant parts, especially on the undersurfaces of leaves and large areas of leaf tissue around the veins. The lesions are pinkish-brown, sometimes concentrically zoned, and may become necrotic .
Witches' broom disease in South America, resulting from the abnormal growth of normally quiescent axillary and terminal buds .

Boll rot characterized by small, rapidly expanding, reddish-brown spots on the capsule, which blacken and are sometimes concentrically zoned. The spots may lead to a coating of thick, grey mycelium on the surface of the affected boll, sometimes with a pink coloration due to conidial masses, followed by production of small black setose fruit bodies as the boll dries out .

diseases.name: cotton blight
symptoms: Small, water-soaked lesions (spots) on leaves that progress into characteristic angular shapes when restricted by leaf veins .
Water-soaked or greasy appearance of the lesions on the underside of the leaf.
Yellow ring or halo around the lesions on the upper leaf surface.
Black cankers on leaf petioles, stems and branches that can cause wilting and death of plant parts above the canker (Blackarm) .
Water-soaked and round lesions on bolls that later become sunken and dark brown or black .

diseases.name: cotton eyespot
symptoms: Cotton verticillium spot disease can infect the cotyledons and true leaves of cotton, stalks, bolls and flower buds, of which the most serious damage to the leaves, in the cotton growth cycle can occur throughout the whole cycle, mostly occurring in the cotton seedling cotyledons and adult leaves. In the young cotyledons, the typical manifestation of the disease is a small brown circular spot, the edge of the spot is purple; in the right conditions, the diameter of the spot can be extended to 10-15 mm; sometimes the petiole will also be affected, yellowish-brown stripes, and in severe cases, the cotyledons dry and fall off. The spots on the leaves in the adult stage are brown dotted spots with purple halos, about 1-2 mm in diameter, and the diameter of the spots can be up to 2-3 cm in serious cases; with the occurrence of the disease, the spots expand to gray-brown suborbicular, dry and fragile, and the necrotic spots may rupture and fall off, forming "holes"; the spots have concentric whorls on both sides, and the spots are more obvious on the front side; when conditions are favorable, the spots will appear yellow-brown stripes, and in serious cases, the cotyledons dry up and fall off. Concentric whorls on both sides of the spot, more obvious on the front side of the leaf; black mold appears on the surface of the spot when the conditions are suitable, i.e. the pathogenic bacteria.

diseases.name: cotton herbicide phytotoxicity
symptoms: Photosynthesis inhibitors: These herbicides block the electron flow that results after the leaf absorbs light energy, causing cell membranes to rupture and plant tissues to die. Symptoms include chlorosis (yellowing) of the leaves between leaf veins or at the margins, followed by necrosis (tissue death) or its paper bag-like appearance. Older leaves are more affected than newer leaves.

Amino acid synthesis inhibitors (ALS inhibitors): These herbicides block the formation of essential amino acids in the plant, leading to protein deficiency and plant death. Symptoms include stunting, chlorosis, reddening or purpling of stems and leaf veins, leaf crinkling or cupping, and necrosis.

Growth regulators (auxin mimics): These herbicides mimic plant hormones that regulate growth and development, causing abnormal cell division and elongation. Symptoms include twisting and curling (epinasty) of stems and petioles, leaf shape distortion (strapping), leaf vein abnormality, stem cracking or splitting, malformed bolls or flowers, and reduced fruit set .

diseases.name: cotton leaf burning disease
symptoms: Small reddish spots with light and dark concentric rings on the lower canopy leaves.
Irregular dead areas with dark brown borders on the upper canopy leaves.
Defoliation of the lower leaves and reduced boll size and quality.

diseases.name: cotton magnesium deficiency
symptoms: Interveinal chlorosis with sharply bordered green veins on mature leaves . The chlorosis can progress to yellowing, reddening, or browning of the leaf margins and blade .
Stunted plant growth, reduced boll retention, delayed flowering, and premature maturity .

diseases.name: cotton potassium deficiency
symptoms: Interveinal chlorosis on older leaves, that eventually progress to a red and/or gold coloration of the leaves .
Necrosis of leaf margins and tips.
Premature defoliation of affected leaves.
Reduced boll size, fiber quality, and yield .

diseases.name: cotton stem blight
symptoms: Wilting of limbs and branches that are infected by the fungus .
Bleaching of stems and bolls that are colonized by the fungus .
Soft rotting of bolls that are invaded by the fungus .

diseases.name: cotton verticillium wilt
symptoms: The main symptom of cotton yellow wilt disease is wilting of the plant, which may be followed by death within a few days.
Other symptoms include yellowing, blighting, and necrosis of the leaves, especially along the veins .
The stems and branches may develop dark brown, elongated, and slightly sunken cankers that may girdle and kill the plant above the lesions.
The root system of affected plants may decay and show a darkened, reddish to wine-colored stain when scraped.

diseases.name: hops downy mildew
symptoms: The main symptoms of hops downy mildew are the so-called spikes, which are systemically infected shoots that arise from infected hop crowns, apical meristems, or lateral branches . The spikes are stunted with shortened internodes and pale green leaves that are brittle and curl upward . The spikes become fragile and desiccate during dry weather .

Another symptom of hops downy mildew is leaf infection, which appears as angular brown spots on the upper surface of the leaves and purple-gray to black downy growth on the lower surface of the leaves . The spots may coalesce and cause leaf distortion and necrosis .

A third symptom of hops downy mildew is cone infection, which occurs when the pathogen infects the leaf-like structures within the cone such as bracts and bracteoles . The infected cones become brown, harden, and are affected in their development and quality .

diseases.name: hops powdery mildew
symptoms: Powdery mildew can cause chlorotic spots or blisters on the upper surface of leaves.
Infected leaves may curl downward or become distorted and necrotic.
Infected cones may show white powdery colonies at the tip or within the bracteoles.
Infected cones may also become brown, distorted, stunted or prematurely ripened.
Infected cones may have reduced alpha-acids content, color defects and off-aromas.

diseases.name: mulberry anthracnose
symptoms: Small, circular, brown or black spots on the leaves, which may coalesce into large necrotic patches .
Premature defoliation of infected leaves, resulting in reduced leaf yield and quality .
Dieback of young shoots and twigs, causing stunted growth and poor branching .

diseases.name: mulberry brown spot
symptoms: Small, circular, brown spots on the leaves, which may coalesce to form large necrotic areas .
Premature defoliation of the infected leaves, resulting in reduced photosynthesis and growth .
Reduced quality and quantity of mulberry leaves and fruits .

diseases.name: mulberry bud leaf white spot disease
symptoms: Small, dark brown and irregular leaf spots with pale-green yellow haloes appear in the spring.
The spots increase in size, the centres becoming whitish and the haloes developing brown necrotic margins (dead edges).
The leaves may crinkle and curl upward, and new shoots may have stunted growth.
The leaf spot lesions may merge to affect larger areas of the leaf.
Premature defoliation and reduced fruit yield may occur after severe attacks.

diseases.name: mulberry sooty blotch
symptoms: Cercospora leaf spot: Small, circular to irregular, brown to black spots on both surfaces of the leaves. The spots may coalesce and cause premature leaf fall .
Phyllosticta leaf spot: Small, circular to angular, brown to black spots with gray centers on both surfaces of the leaves. The spots may have yellow halos and cause leaf distortion and defoliation .

Cerotelium leaf rust: Small, yellowish to orange pustules on the lower surface of the leaves. The pustules may rupture and release powdery spores that can infect other leaves. The upper surface of the leaves may show yellow spots or blotches. The infected leaves may curl, dry up, and fall off .

diseases.name: peanut anthracnose
symptoms: This disease are brown lesions on leaves, stems, flowers, and fruits.
The lesions may expand and coalesce, causing leaf distortion, wilting, defoliation, fruit rotting, and premature senescence .

diseases.name: peanut black spot disease
symptoms: Symptoms of this disease are brown to black spots on the leaves, stems, pegs, and pods of peanut plants .
The spots are irregular in shape and size and may coalesce to form large blotches that cover most of the leaf surface.

The spots may also have a web-like appearance due to the presence of fungal mycelium on the leaf surface. The infected leaves may turn yellow and fall off prematurely, resulting in defoliation and reduced photosynthesis .
The infected pegs and pods may become shriveled and discolored, affecting the quality and quantity of the seeds.

diseases.name: peanut black spot mixed with net blotch
symptoms: These diseases are changes in color, shape or function of the plant parts affected by the pathogens.
Peanut black spot causes brown to black spots on the leaves and stems that may have yellow halos .
The spots can coalesce and cause premature defoliation and reduced pod yield .
Net blotch causes brown to black spots on the leaves and stems that may have yellow halos . The spots can enlarge and form irregular patches that reduce photosynthesis and transpiration .
Both diseases can also affect the pegs and pods and cause reduced quality and quantity of seeds .

diseases.name: peanut black spot mixed with scorch spot
symptoms: Early leaf spot: Brown to dark brown, circular spots with a distinct yellow border or halo on the upper leaf surface; brown spots on the lower leaf surface; defoliation of diseased leaflets.
Late leaf spot: Black, circular spots with or without a yellow halo on the upper leaf surface; dark brown to black spots on the lower leaf surface; defoliation of diseased leaflets.
Scorch spot: Small, irregularly shaped brown spots with dark margins on both leaf surfaces; spots may coalesce to form large necrotic areas; defoliation of diseased leaflets.

diseases.name: peanut brown spot disease
symptoms: This disease are brown or black spots on the leaves and stems of the peanut plants.
The spots may have a yellow halo around them and can coalesce to form large necrotic areas.
The spots can penetrate through the leaf and are visible on both surfaces.
The infected leaves may turn yellow, curl up, and fall off prematurely.
The defoliation can reduce photosynthesis, pod formation, and yield .

diseases.name: peanut iron deficiency
symptoms: Iron deficiency in peanut plants are chlorosis, or yellowing, of the young leaves, especially along the veins.
The chlorosis can spread to the whole plant and cause growth retardation and reduced yield.

diseases.name: peanut leaf scorch
symptoms: This disease are pepper spot and leaf scorch spot .

Pepper spot is characterized by small (1-2 mm), circular, dark brown to black lesions on both surfaces of the leaves . Leaf scorch spot is characterized by larger (3-10 mm), irregular, brown to tan lesions with dark brown margins on both surfaces of the leaves .
Both types of lesions can coalesce and cause extensive necrosis and defoliation of the leaves .

diseases.name: peanut magnesium deficiency
symptoms: Peanut magnesium deficiency are yellowing of older leaves, interveinal chlorosis, necrotic spots, and premature defoliation .
These symptoms may reduce the photosynthetic capacity and yield potential of the plant.

diseases.name: peanut net blotch mixed with brown spot
symptoms: This disease are brown or black spots on the upper leaf surface that enlarge to become circular spots with a distinct yellow border or halo (early leaf spot) or lack a yellow halo (late leaf spot).
The spots can reach a size of about one-fourth of an inch in diameter.
The spots are also clearly defined on the lower leaf surface, where they are usually brown (early leaf spot) or dark brown to black (late leaf spot).
The spots can coalesce and cause blotch symptoms on the leaves.
The infection also causes hormonal changes in the leaf that lead to leaf drop or defoliation.
Defoliation reduces healthy leaf area and weakens the stems and pegs causing pods to fall off the vine during digging and harvest.

diseases.name: peanut ozone damage
symptoms: Ozone damage are mainly manifested as leaf injuries that appear as chlorotic or necrotic spots, flecks, stipples or blotches on the upper surface of the leaves.
The symptoms usually start from the older leaves and progress to the younger ones with prolonged exposure.
The severity of the symptoms depends on the ozone concentration, exposure duration, plant species, cultivar and environmental conditions.
For peanut, ozone damage can cause reduced leaf area, premature leaf senescence and leaf abscission.

diseases.name: peanut phosphorus deficiency
symptoms: Phosphorus deficiency in peanuts are slow growth, short plants, few branches, poorly developed roots, dark green or reddish purple color along the edge of the lower plant leaves, reduced flower bud differentiation, small pods, deformed fruit, and late maturity .

diseases.name: peanut powdery mildew
symptoms: Small, round, whitish or gray spots on the upper surface of leaves .
Yellowing and drying of leaves .
Twisting, breaking, or distortion of leaves, buds, and growing tips .
Reduced plant growth, flowering, and fruiting .

diseases.name: peanut rust disease
symptoms: Orange to reddish brown pustules on the lower leaf surface, usually surrounded by a yellow halo .
Premature defoliation and reduced pod yield and quality .
Reduced photosynthesis and translocation of assimilates to pods.

diseases.name: peanut scab disease
symptoms: Lesions or scabs on the leaves, petioles, stems, roots, and pods of peanut. The lesions are initially small, circular, and light brown, and later become larger, irregular, and dark brown or black. The lesions can coalesce and cover large areas of the plant tissue.

Necrosis and hyperplasia of the infected tissue. The necrotic tissue becomes dry and brittle, and the hyperplastic tissue becomes thickened and distorted. The infected pods may crack open and expose the seeds to infection.
Reduced growth and yield of peanut plants. The disease can cause significant losses in peanut production and quality, especially when infection occurs early in the season or during pod formation.

diseases.name: sesame leaf blight
symptoms: The infected seeds become discolored and shriveled and fail to germinate or produce weak seedlings.
The infected seedlings show water-soaked lesions on the stem near the soil line and collapse within a few days.
The infected stems show dark brown to black lesions that girdle the stem and cause wilting and death of the plant.

The infected leaves show circular to irregular brown spots with concentric rings that coalesce to form large necrotic areas. The spots may have yellow halos or reddish margins. The infected leaves may curl, dry up, and fall off prematurely .
The infected pods show brown spots that enlarge and cover the entire pod surface. The pods may crack open and drop the seeds or produce shriveled and discolored seeds .

diseases.name: sesame leaf spot disease
symptoms: There are two common symptoms of leaf disease, a leaf spot is mostly 1~3 mm in diameter round spots, gray-white in the middle, surrounded by purple-brown, gray mold on the back of the spot. Severe occurrences, multiple spots can be fused into large plaques, easy to dry and rupture, often causing defoliation. Another leaf spot for snake eye-like spots, the middle of a gray-white dots, like an eyeball, that is, "snake eye spot", surrounded by light gray, peripheral yellow-brown, round to indeterminate, cross diameter of 3 ~ 10 mm. Stem onset spots brown, irregular shape, high humidity when the disease dark gray mold.

diseases.name: sesame yellow dwarf virus disease
symptoms: stunting, yellowing, leaf curling, and reduced seed production.

diseases.name: sugarcane red rot
symptoms: Drooping, withering, yellowing and drying of the upper leaves
Wilting and dying of the entire crown and plant
Blackening and death of the eyes (buds) at the nodes
Splitting of the stem and oozing of a gummy dark-red material from the fibro-vascular bundles near the base
Formation of irregular discoloured blotches on the stem that may be reddish, yellowish or white with red margins
Shriveling and dulling of the rind with black specks on it
Sour smell from the split cane with red tissue and white cross-bands

diseases.name: sunflower bacterial leaf spot
symptoms: Necrotic spots of varying size and shape on the leaves, which may be surrounded by chlorotic halos . The spots are water-soaked and angular at first, and later become dry and darkened.
Coalescing lesions that cause tissues to dry, die and fall out, often in linear strips between leaf veins.
Systemic infection that spreads along veins, but without the chlorotic bleaching of plant tissues.
Foliar death and defoliation, which can reduce photosynthesis and yield potential.

diseases.name: sunflower black spot
symptoms: Dark circular spots on the leaves, sometimes with yellow halos or borders. The spots may coalesce and cause the leaves to wither and die .
Dark streaks, flecks or diamond-shaped spots on the stems or petioles. The spots may girdle the stem and cause it to break .
Dark spots on the back of the head, making the plant look wilted .

diseases.name: tea algal leaf spot
symptoms: Grey, green or tan spots or blotches on the leaves, especially on the upper surface
Spots may coalesce and cover large areas of the leaf
Spots may have a velvety or crusty appearance due to the algal growth
Spots may cause leaf distortion, curling, or necrosis
In severe cases, spots may spread to branches and fruits

diseases.name: tea anthracnose
symptoms: Brown or black spots or blotches on the leaves, especially on the lower surface
Spots may coalesce and cover large areas of the leaf
Spots may have a sunken or raised appearance due to the fungal growth
Spots may cause leaf distortion, curling, wilting, or necrosis
In severe cases, spots may spread to stems and buds

diseases.name: tea bird eye spot
symptoms: Brown or black spots or blotches on the leaves, especially on the lower surface
Spots may coalesce and cover large areas of the leaf
Spots may have a sunken or raised appearance due to the fungal growth
Spots may cause leaf distortion, curling, wilting, or necrosis
In severe cases, spots may spread to stems and buds

diseases.name: tea brown blight
symptoms: Brown or black spots or blotches on the leaves, especially on the lower surface
Spots may coalesce and cover large areas of the leaf
Spots may have a sunken or raised appearance due to the fungal growth
Spots may cause leaf distortion, curling, wilting, or necrosis
In severe cases, spots may spread to stems and buds

diseases.name: tea gray light
symptoms: According to and , some common symptoms of tea gray light disease are:
Small, oval, pale yellow-green spots on young leaves, often surrounded by a narrow yellow zone.
Spots enlarge and turn brown or gray with concentric rings and scattered tiny black dots (pycnidia) on the surface.
Dried tissue falls off, leaving shot holes on the leaves.
Severe infection leads to defoliation, reduced yield, and poor quality.

diseases.name: tea red leaf spot
symptoms: The disease occurs in all tea regions of the country, mainly affecting the leaves. Young leaves, adult leaves, old leaves infected, more from the tip or edge of the leaf began to produce light brown spots, and then expanded to half or all the leaves, forming irregularly shaped large spots, the color of the spot is more consistent, dark reddish brown to reddish brown, the edge of the edge of the dark brown elevated line, and the healthy part of the boundary is very obvious, the later part of the disease was born a slightly raised black dots, that is, the pathogenic bacterial conidiophores.

diseases.name: tea white spot disease
symptoms: The appearance of small, circular, pale green or yellowish spots on the upper surface of young leaves. These spots gradually enlarge and become white or grayish-white with a velvety texture.
The infected leaves curl upwards along the margins and become distorted and brittle.
The lower surface of the infected leaves shows a brownish or blackish discoloration due to the presence of fungal mycelium and spores.
The infection may also spread to young shoots, buds and flowers, causing them to wither and drop off.

diseases.name: tobacco frog eye disease
symptoms: Small, circular or irregular, sunken, brown patches on the lower and middle leaves of the plant .
White centers and dark brown margins of the patches, resembling frog eyes .
Yellow halos around the patches on green leaves.
Coalescence of patches causing drying and withering of leaves .

diseases.name: tobacco leaf spot disease
symptoms: Cercospora nicotianae causes round or oval lesions with a dark brown margin and a pale tan/white center, sometimes with a yellow halo around them.
Rhizoctonia solani causes large lesions with alternating rings of light and dark brown, resembling a target.
Alternaria alternata causes irregular-shaped lesions that are dark brown and may coalesce to form large necrotic areas.

diseases.name: tobacco mosaic disease
symptoms: Target spot: Circular to irregular brown lesions with concentric rings on leaves and stems; lesions may coalesce and cause leaf blight; defoliation may occur.
Frogeye: Small circular brown lesions with light brown centers on leaves; lesions may have yellow halos; lesions may coalesce and cause leaf blight; defoliation may occur.

Blue mold: Pale green to yellow spots on upper leaf surface; grayish-white downy growth on lower leaf surface; spots may enlarge and coalesce; leaves may curl and wilt; systemic infection may cause stunting and distortion of plants.
Black shank: Yellowing and wilting of leaves; dark brown to black lesion at the base of the stem; blackening of pith with distinct disks and white hyphae when cut open.
Pythium root rot: Browning and rotting of roots; stunting and wilting of plants; damping-off of seedlings.

diseases.name: tobacco mosaic virus
symptoms: Mosaic: A blotchy pattern of light and dark green areas on the leaves, giving a mosaic-like appearance.
Mottling: A yellowish or pale green discoloration of the leaves, often intermixed with normal green color.
Distortion: A curling, cupping, elongation (strapping), roughening, wrinkling and other growth distortions of the leaves and fruits.
Stunting: A reduced growth and size of the whole plant or parts of it.

diseases.name: tobacco scorch disease
symptoms: Necrotic lesions: The infected leaves develop brown or black spots or patches that may coalesce and form irregular patterns. The lesions may also appear on the stems, petioles, and flowers.
Chlorosis: The infected leaves show yellowing or bleaching of the tissues around the necrotic lesions or along the veins. The chlorosis may spread to the whole leaf or plant.
Wilting: The infected plants show wilting or drooping of the leaves and stems due to the reduced water uptake and transpiration. The wilting may be more pronounced during hot and dry conditions.
Stunting: The infected plants show reduced growth and development due to the impaired photosynthesis and respiration. The plants may also produce fewer and smaller flowers and fruits.

diseases.name: tobacco virus disease
symptoms: Mosaic: Irregular patches of light green, yellow or white on the leaves, giving a mosaic-like appearance.
Mottling: Blotches or spots of different shades of green on the leaves.
Distortion: Abnormal growth or shape of the leaves, stems or flowers, such as curling, crinkling, twisting or stunting.
Necrosis: Death of plant tissues, resulting in brown or black spots or lesions on the leaves, stems or flowers.
Wilting: Loss of turgor pressure in plant cells, causing drooping or collapsing of the leaves or stems.
Chlorosis: Loss of chlorophyll in plant cells, causing yellowing or whitening of the leaves or stems.

diseases.name: apple black rot
symptoms: small purple spots appear on the upper surface of the leaves in early spring, which enlarge to form circular lesions with purple margins and brown centers. The lesions may coalesce and cause leaf distortion, chlorosis, and premature defoliation . This phase of the disease is also known as frogeye leaf spot.

dark brown to black lesions appear on the fruit surface, usually near the calyx end. The lesions expand and cover most of the fruit, causing it to shrivel and mummify. The infected fruits may remain attached to the tree or drop prematurely . The fungus may produce black pycnidia (spore-bearing structures) on the surface of the lesions.

reddish-brown to black cankers develop on the bark of infected branches or twigs. The cankers are slightly sunken and may girdle and kill the affected limbs. The fungus may also produce black pycnidia on the surface of the cankers .

diseases.name: apple brown spot disease
symptoms: Apple scab: The signs on leaves and fruits are velvety spores that appear on the surface of the spots or lesions. These spores are usually olive-green to black in color and can be seen with a magnifying glass .

Black rot: The signs on branches are orange droplets that ooze from the cankers. These droplets contain bacterial cells and can be seen with the naked eye. The signs on fruits are small black spots that appear on the rotten areas. These spots are fungal structures called pycnidia that produce spores and can be seen with a magnifying glass.
Acremonium brown spot: The signs on fruits are white to pink fungal growth that covers the brown spots. This fungal growth produces spores called conidia that can be seen with a microscope.

diseases.name: apple cedar-apple rust fungus
symptoms: small yellow spots appear on the upper surface of the leaves in late spring, which enlarge and turn orange-red with black dots in the center. The spots may coalesce and cause leaf distortion, chlorosis, and premature defoliation .
similar spots on leaves appear on the fruit surface, which may crack and deform the fruit. The infected fruits may drop prematurely or remain on the tree with reduced quality .

diseases.name: apple gray spot disease
symptoms: Black or gray blotches or speckles on the fruit surface, which may make the fruit look unappealing and reduce its shelf life .
No symptoms on the leaves or shoots, unlike other fungal diseases such as apple scab or powdery mildew.

diseases.name: apple herbicide phytotoxicity
symptoms: Leaf distortions, such as cupping, curling, twisting, or narrowing .
Leaf discoloration, such as chlorosis (yellowing), necrosis (browning), or reddening .
Leaf drop, especially of young leaves .
Shoot dieback, especially of terminal shoots .
Reduced fruit set, size, or quality .
Bark cracking, splitting, or cankering .

diseases.name: apple leaflet disease
symptoms: This disease are visible effects caused by the plant's reaction to the pathogen.
They include changes in color, shape, or function of the leaves and fruits.
On the leaves, the symptoms appear as circular or irregular brown or black spots with a velvety surface .
The spots may coalesce and cause deformation or curling of the leaves. The infected leaves may also turn yellow and fall prematurely .
On the fruits, the symptoms appear as brown or black scabs with a corky texture on the skin .
The scabs may crack open and expose the flesh to secondary infections. The infected fruits may also be deformed or drop early .

diseases.name: apple nitrogen deficiency
symptoms: Apple Nitrogen Deficiency are pale green to yellow leaves that develop uniformly on the leaf with no patterning or mottling, and small leaf size.
The symptoms first appear on older leaves, since nitrogen is mobile in trees and moves to younger leaves.

The leaves may drop early in the fall. The shoot growth is reduced, and the twigs are thin. The fruit set may be light with considerable June drop. The fruits are smaller and often color and mature early.

diseases.name: apple phosphorus deficiency
symptoms: Phosphorus deficiency in apples are reduced shoot growth, dark-red branches and leaves, small and poorly colored fruits, and low temperature breakdown in storage .
Low temperature breakdown is a condition where brown and soft areas appear within the flesh of the fruits in storage.

diseases.name: apple powdery mildew
symptoms: Delayed opening of infected buds in spring
Whitish fuzzy lesions on leaves, blossoms, and fruit
Curling and distortion of leaves
Abnormal development of flowers
Russeting and dwarfing of fruit

diseases.name: apple round spot disease
symptoms: On leaves: dull, olive-green to brown spots that may grow into large circles with a velvety surface; leaves may become dwarfed, twisted, yellow, or fall off prematurely .
On fruits: olive-green to black spots that may develop into lesions with a rough, wart-like, or corky surface; fruits may crack open, drop early, or become deformed and unfit for eating .

diseases.name: apple rust
symptoms: On apple and crabapple trees: Bright orange to red leaf spots that may coalesce and cause premature leaf drop; yellow or orange spots or bands on the fruits that may crack or deform; reduced fruit quality and yield.

On junipers and red cedars: Woody galls on the branches that may cause witches' brooms or swollen stems; orange gelatinous horns that emerge from the galls during wet weather and release spores; branch dieback or reduced vigor.

diseases.name: apple scab disease
symptoms: Olive-green to brown spots with fringed margins, up to 0.5 inch in diameter, often along leaf veins or near leaf margins. Spots may coalesce and form large necrotic areas. Infected leaves may curl, twist, yellow, and drop prematurely.

Olive-green to black spots with corky or cracked surfaces, up to 0.5 inch in diameter, often near fruit calyx or stem end. Spots may coalesce and deform fruits. Infected fruits may crack, shrivel, or drop prematurely.

diseases.name: apple spotted leaf litter
symptoms: On leaves: dull, olive-green round spots that gradually grow into brown or black large circles with a velvet-like surface up to half an inch across; dwarfed or twisted leaves that turn yellow and drop prematurely .

On fruits: slightly raised, black spots with a rough, wart-like or corky surface, and cracks in the fruit; deformed and unappealing fruits that may drop before harvest or show symptoms during storage .

diseases.name: banana leaf spot
symptoms: Banana Leaf Spot manifests as small, dark spots on the banana leaves.
As the disease progresses, these spots may enlarge and merge, forming extensive brown or black lesions. Infected leaves may exhibit yellowing and wilting.

diseases.name: bean angular leaf spot
symptoms: Leaves develop small, angular, brown or straw-colored spots with a yellow halo. The spots are confined within the leaf veins and do not cross over. The spots may dry out and drop out, leaving irregular holes in the leaves .
Fruits develop small, circular, water-soaked spots that may turn chalky white and crack open. The cracks may allow secondary infections that cause fruit rot .

diseases.name: bean bacterial blight
symptoms: Leaf symptoms: Small water-soaked spots that become necrotic (brown or reddish) lesions of irregular shape with distinct yellow margins or halos. The lesions may enlarge, coalesce, and fall out giving the leaves a tattered appearance. The disease may become systemic and cause yellowing and death of new foliage.

Pod symptoms: Similar water-soaked spots that enlarge into reddish brown lesions on the pod surface. The lesions may have a yellow or cream-colored bacterial exudate under moist conditions. The pods may become bent or twisted at the infection site. The seeds inside may abort or shrivel and discolor as they mature.

diseases.name: bean powdery mildew
symptoms: Small and white talcum-like spots on the upper surface of leaves .
Gradual enlargement and coalescence of the spots to form a whitish, powdery growth over the leaves, stems, and pods .
Downward curling, yellowing, browning, drying, and falling off of the infected leaves .
Twisting, buckling, or distortion of the leaves in some varieties .
Reduced yield, shortened production period, and plant death in severe cases .

diseases.name: bean rust
symptoms: Leaves and pods develop pustules of red-brown powdery spores (urediniospores) that rupture the epidermis. They are often surrounded by a yellow halo .
Severely infected leaves turn chlorotic, dry and decline. They may drop off, resulting in defoliation .
Stems and branches may also develop pustules of spores, but less frequently than leaves and pods.

diseases.name: blueberry iron deficiency disease
symptoms: Iron deficiency causes chlorosis, or yellowing, of the leaves, especially the young leaves . The yellowing is interveinal, meaning that the veins remain green while the rest of the leaf turns yellow . This is because iron is involved in chlorophyll production, which gives the leaves their green color. Iron deficiency can also cause stunted growth, reduced yield, and in some cases, death of the plant.

diseases.name: blueberry leaf spot disease
symptoms: Anthracnose: Small reddish flecks on young leaves and stems; large brown to gray lesions on leaves with a dark border; stem lesions that are dark red, circular to elliptical, and can cause dieback.

Septoria: Small spots on leaves with a white to tan center and a purple border; black fungal fruiting bodies in the center of lesions; stem lesions that are flat or slightly sunken with a tan to gray center and a reddish brown margin.

Double spot: Small spots on leaves that enlarge in late summer; secondary necrotic area around the original spot spreading in an irregular to fan-shaped pattern; dark border surrounding the original spot persists.
Gloeocercospora: Small to medium dark brown, circular to angular lesions on leaves surrounded by a dark border; no fungal fruiting bodies visible.

Leaf rust: Pale green spots on the upper surface of leaves with yellow-orange pustules on the lower surface; pustules release powdery spores that can re-infect leaves; severe infections can cause premature leaf drop.

Exobasidium: Pale green spots on the upper surface of leaves with pure white fungal growth on the lower surface; infected flowers produce malformed berries that are pale green or pinkish-red with white fungal growth.

diseases.name: blueberry red ringspot virus disease
symptoms: The most characteristic symptom of this disease is the appearance of red ringspots on the stems, leaves, and ripening fruit of infected plants . The ringspots are usually circular or oval, 1 to 5 mm in diameter, and may have a yellow halo around them . The ringspots may coalesce and form larger lesions on the stems and leaves.
The disease may also cause stunting, reduced vigor, leaf drop, and reduced yield and fruit quality in severely infected plants . The fruit may become malformed, cracked, or shriveled.

diseases.name: cabbage phytotoxicity
symptoms: Cutting off the infected parts: This can help reduce the spread of diseases and pests by removing the source of inoculum or infestation. For example, cutting off the infected leaves can help control alternaria leaf spot and downy mildew.
Hand picking: This can help reduce the population of pests by manually removing them from the plants. For example, hand picking can help control caterpillars and snails.

diseases.name: cauliflower bacterial spot rot
symptoms: Water-soaked spots on the leaves and/or developing heads that become enlarged and sunken with time .
Infected tissue that becomes mushy and discolored, ranging from cream to black .
Seepage from affected areas and a strong, decomposing odor .
Wilting and collapse of plant parts such as stems and roots .

diseases.name: cauliflower black rot
symptoms: Irregular, dull, yellow blotches that appear on the edges of leaves and expand into V-shaped areas with the wide part of the “V” at the edge of the leaf and the point of the “V” toward the attachment point of the leaf to the plant. The V-shaped areas are initially yellow, but eventually become brown and necrotic in the center with a yellow border or halo.
Veins in affected areas become dark brown or black, forming a net-like pattern.
Interior stem tissue becomes brown or black.
Unilateral stunting of some leaves occurs.
Wilting symptoms due to vascular tissue damage.

diseases.name: cauliflower downy mildew
symptoms: According to , the symptoms of downy mildew on cauliflower include yellow spots on the upper leaf surfaces and gray mold on the lower leaf surfaces. The spots may coalesce and turn brown or necrotic, causing the leaves to wither and die. The mold may produce spores that can be dispersed by wind or water. The disease can also affect the cauliflower heads, causing dark brown internal streaks or rots.

diseases.name: celery bacterial leaf blight
symptoms: Water-soaked spots on the leaves that become bright yellow and then brown with a yellow halo. The spots are usually angular or rectangular and limited by leaf veins. They may dry out and become papery and tan .
The spots may coalesce and cause blighting of the foliage, especially under moist conditions .
The disease usually affects the older leaves that are protected by the plant canopy, except where sprinkler irrigation is used .
The disease rarely affects the leaf petioles, but when it does, it causes a brown discoloration over the entire petiole, which is called brown stem.

diseases.name: celery bacterial leaf spot
symptoms: Small, water-soaked spots on both sides of the leaf that are usually limited by leaf veins and have an angular, square, or rectangular appearance .
Yellowing of the spots that gradually turn into brown, necrotic spots with a yellow halo .
Blighting of the foliage if the spots coalesce and cause extensive tissue death .
The symptoms usually appear first on the older leaves that are protected by the plant canopy, except where sprinkler irrigation is used.

diseases.name: celery black spot disease
symptoms: On leaves: light brown, circular to mildly angular, lesions that may appear oily or greasy and may be accompanied by yellow halos. The lesions may also have gray fungal growth. The leaf spots dry out and leaf tissue becomes papery, often splitting and cracking. On petioles and stems: long, brown to gray lesions that may become sunken and dark brown .

diseases.name: celery leaf spot disease
symptoms: Cercospora blight: Light brown, circular to mildly angular lesions on the leaves that may appear oily or greasy and may be surrounded by yellow halos. The lesions may also have gray fungal growth on the undersides. The leaf spots dry out and leaf tissue becomes papery, often splitting and cracking. Long, brown to gray lesions on the petioles.

Septoria blight: Round yellow lesions on the leaves that coalesce and cause the leaves to become dry and papery. Tiny, dark specks (spores) in the damaged tissue that may produce jelly-like threads during damp weather. The disease affects older, lower foliage first, then moves up to younger leaves. The disease also affects stems and can ruin entire celery plants.

diseases.name: celery phyllosticta leaf spot
symptoms: This disease are circular or irregular brown spots with reddish or black borders on the leaves, which may coalesce and cause leaf blight or drop.
The spots may also have black fruiting bodies (pycnidia) in the center or along the margins .

diseases.name: celery phytotoxicity
symptoms: Wilting and yellowing of leaves and stems
Water-soaked lesions on stems and petioles that enlarge and become soft and rotten
Collapse of stems and lodging of plants
White cottony mycelium on infected tissues
Black sclerotia inside or outside the stems

diseases.name: celery spot blight
symptoms: On leaves: Light brown, circular to mildly angular lesions that may appear oily or greasy and may have yellow halos. The lesions may also have gray fungal growth. The leaf spots dry out and leaf tissue becomes papery, often splitting and cracking .
On petioles: Long, brown to gray lesions that may affect the quality and yield of celery .

diseases.name: cherry bacterial perforation disease
symptoms: Leaf spots: Brown or reddish-brown spots on leaves that may drop out, leaving small “shot holes” .

Leaf wilting and yellowing: Leaves and shoot growth beyond a canker may wilt and die during the growing season when cankers girdle a branch or the trunk. Infected cherry tree leaves will turn completely yellow and drop from the trees in mid-summer if the infection is severe .
Fruit lesions: Deep, black depressions on fruit or fruit stems .
Cankers: Darkened, sunken areas on branches, twigs, or trunks that may ooze gum .

diseases.name: cherry brown spot perforation disease
symptoms: Blossom blight: The flowers collapse and brown quickly. Small cankers form on twigs and gum oozes out .

Fruit rot: The ripening fruits brown and shrivel as they become covered with tan to gray masses of spores. The fruits may crack and leak juice. Some fruits may fall while others remain as mummies on the tree .
Twig blight: The infected twigs die back and show brown lesions with gray spore masses. The bark may split and exude gum .

diseases.name: cherry mosaic virus disease
symptoms: Yellow, white or green stripes/ streaks/ spots on foliage
Wrinkled, curled, or small leaves
Pronounced yellowing only of veins
Stunted growth and reduced yields
Infected fruit appears mottled and develops raised “warty” areas
Dark green blisters on stems that dry out rapidly
Enation, or outgrowths, on the bottom surface of leaves
Deformed fruit that is angular or flattened
Delayed ripening of fruit or uneven ripening
Distorted leaf growth, including twisted leaf tips

diseases.name: cherry powdery mildew
symptoms: Superficial, white, weblike growth on leaves, shoots, or fruit .
Light roughly-circular patches on young leaves.
Distortion, curling, puckering, twisting or dropping of infected leaves .
Reduced size, pale color or deformation of new leaves on new shoots.
Unmarketable fruit due to the covering of white fungal growth on the cherry surface.

diseases.name: chinar leaf spot
symptoms: Dark brown to black spots on the leaves, often with a yellow halo around them. The spots may coalesce and form large blotches that cover most of the leaf surface.
Premature defoliation of infected leaves, especially during severe infections. This can reduce the ornamental value and photosynthetic capacity of the plant.
Reduced growth and vigor of the plant due to loss of foliage and reduced photosynthesis.

diseases.name: chinese cabbage anthracnose
symptoms: Discolored lesions on the cotyledons that turn into necrotic spots on seedlings.
Small, angular, light green to yellow lesions on the upper leaf surface that enlarge and form irregularly-shaped necrotic patches on older leaves .

Pale brown to gray discoloration on the surface of the curd or head, and grayish or black spots on the stem beneath. The interior of the curd or head will become dark brown to black or gray under severe infection.
Dark, discolored zones that spread from the crown into the taproot of turnip and radish.

diseases.name: chinese cabbage bacterial black spot
symptoms: Yellow or chlorotic patches on the leaf margins that form V-shaped lesions along the veins.
Brown or black necrotic tissue on the leaf surface or inside the head.
Wilting, drooping, and dying of leaves and stems.
Blackening and rotting of veins and vascular tissues.

diseases.name: chinese cabbage bacterial brown spot
symptoms: Bacterial leaf spot can cause spots to form on the leaves that start out small, becoming dark brown or purple as they increase in size .
These spots can join together to form angular lesions, which make the leaves look ragged .
In the case of a severe infection, the foliage can die .

diseases.name: chinese cabbage black rot
symptoms: The disease symptoms of black rot are irregular, dull, yellow blotches that appear on the edges of leaves. These blotches expand into V-shaped areas with the wide part of the “V” at the edge of the leaf and the point of the “V” toward the attachment point of the leaf to the plant. The V-shaped areas are initially yellow, but eventually become brown and necrotic (i.e., dead) in the center with a yellow border or halo. The affected leaves may also droop and die as the disease progresses.
Black rot can affect cruciferous plants at any stage of growth, from germination to heading. The disease may also cause wilting, stunting, and poor head formation in infected plants .
Diseased plants may rot quickly before or after harvest because of secondary infection from bacterial soft-rot.

diseases.name: chinese cabbage black spot
symptoms: The disease causes yellow spots that grow larger and develop rings around them like a target or bull's-eye . These spots are usually surrounded by a halo of yellow discoloration.
As the tissue dies, the centers may fall out, resulting in holes in the foliage .
As the disease develops, the spots join together to form large areas of dead tissue .
The disease can also infect the base of the developing heads, causing black spots and stripes along the leaves and black sooty growth inside the heads .

diseases.name: chinese cabbage blight
symptoms: According to , some of the disease symptoms of cabbage blight are:
Yellow spots that grow larger and develop rings around them like a target or bull鈥檚-eye on the leaves.
Holes in the foliage as the tissue dies and falls out.
Large areas of dead tissue as the spots join together.
According to , some more disease symptoms of cabbage blight are:
A lifeless, yellowish green color overall, but often more noticeable on one side of the plant.
A lateral warping or curling of the stem and leaves.
The lower part of the leaf blade adjoining the petiole or midrib wilts and dies first, resulting in a curve in the midrib.
The lower leaves turn yellow first, and then symptoms move to the upper leaves. With time, the yellow turns brown, and the tissue becomes dry and brittle.

diseases.name: chinese cabbage grey spot disease
symptoms: Yellow spots that grow larger and develop rings around them like a target or bull -eye.
As the tissue dies, the centers may fall out, resulting in holes in the foliage.
As the disease develops, the spots join together to form large areas of dead tissue.
The fungus can also infect the base of the developing heads, causing them to rot or become discolored.

diseases.name: chinese cabbage magnesium deficiency disease
symptoms: Interveinal chlorosis means that the regions between the leaf veins turn yellow-green, orange, or red, while the veins remain green or white. Leaf curling means that the interveinal zones curl up.
These symptoms usually start on older leaves and progress to younger leaves as the deficiency worsens.

diseases.name: chinese cabbage phytotoxicity
symptoms: Aphids: They cause yellowing, curling, wilting, or stunting of the leaves. They also produce honeydew, a sticky substance that attracts ants and promotes fungal growth.
Cabbage maggots: They cause wilting, yellowing, or dying of the plants. They also create tunnels and holes in the roots and stems.
Caterpillars: They cause holes, notches, or skeletonization of the leaves. They also damage the heads by boring into them or leaving frass (excrement) behind.
Flea beetles: They cause small round holes or pits on the leaves. They also reduce plant vigor and yield.
Slugs and snails: They cause irregular holes or ragged edges on the leaves. They also leave slimy trails behind.
Spider mites: They cause yellowing, bronzing, or speckling of the leaves. They also create fine webbing on the undersides of the leaves.
Stink bugs: They cause yellowing or browning spots on the leaves and heads. They also inject toxins that affect plant growth and quality.
Whiteflies: They cause yellowing, curling, wilting, or stunting of the leaves. They also produce honeydew and transmit viral diseases.
Wireworms: They cause wilting or dying of seedlings. They also create holes in the roots and stems.

diseases.name: chinese cabbage virus disease
symptoms: Yellow rings or spots on young leaves, mottled or mosaic patterns of light and dark areas on older leaves. Leaf distortion, curling, blistering, or stunting necrotic or dead patches on leaves premature senescence or wilting of plants reduced yield and quality of crops .

diseases.name: chinese cabbage white spot disease
symptoms: White leaf spots: These are circular to irregular spots that range from Â¼ to Â½ inch (6 mm. to 1 cm.) across and scattered across the leaf. They start as light tan, dry spots and soon turn to papery white lesions on the leaf surrounded by a halo of yellow or pale green. The spots grow and merge, reducing the chlorophyll production and causing the leaf to yellow and die. The spots may also form on the base of the developing heads.
Holes in the foliage: As the tissue dies, the centers of the spots may fall out, resulting in holes in the foliage. This makes the leaves look ragged and unappealing.

diseases.name: chinese toon bacterial brown spot disease
symptoms: Leaf spots: Small, circular, water-soaked spots appear on both sides of the leaves, which later turn brown and necrotic. The spots may coalesce and cause leaf blight and defoliation.
Shoot blight: Young shoots show wilting, browning and dieback symptoms. The bark may crack and ooze bacterial exudates.
Canker: Dark brown to black lesions form on the stems and branches, which may girdle and kill the affected parts. The cankers may also exude bacterial slime.

diseases.name: chinese toon brown spot disease
symptoms: The most visible symptom of brown spot disease of Toona is the appearance of brown spots or lesions on the leaves . These spots may vary in size, shape, and color depending on the plant species, age, and environmental conditions. They may be circular, oval, irregular, or angular; small or large; light brown, dark brown, reddish brown, or purple brown; with or without a yellow margin or a gray center . The spots may coalesce and cause extensive necrosis or blighting of the leaves .

Another symptom of brown spot disease of Toona is the formation of brown spots or cankers on the stems, twigs, and branches . These spots may be sunken, elongated, or cracked; with or without gum exudation or fungal fruiting bodies . The spots may girdle the stems or branches and cause wilting or dieback .
A less common symptom of brown spot disease of Toona is the infection of flowers and fruits . The flowers may show brown spots or blight; while the fruits may show brown spots or rot .

diseases.name: chinese toon mixture of powdery mildew and rust
symptoms: Powdery mildew: The most characteristic symptom of powdery mildew is the presence of whitish-gray, talcum-like powdery patches of fungal growth on the upper or lower leaf surfaces, stems, flowers, or fruits . These patches may coalesce to cover large areas of the plant surface. The infected tissues may show little reaction at first, but later they may become stunted, distorted, discolored (yellow or purple), wilted, or die . Powdery mildew may also affect plant growth, flowering, fruiting, and quality .

Rust: The most characteristic symptom of rust is the presence of orange or brown pustules (uredinia) on the lower leaf surfaces or stems of infected plants. These pustules contain masses of spores (urediniospores) that can be easily rubbed off. The infected tissues may also develop yellow spots (chlorosis) on the upper leaf surfaces corresponding to the pustules below. Rust may cause premature leaf drop, reduced photosynthesis, weakened plant vigor, reduced yield and quality, and increased susceptibility to other pests or diseases.

diseases.name: chinese toon rust
symptoms: According to , the signature symptom of rust is the orange, yellow, brown, or red spore masses on the outside of the plant. These spores appear as raised dots on the leaves or stems of the plant. Some types of rust form galls that then become covered in the spore masses. Rust occasionally forms on blooms. Symptoms occur from the ground up. They are usually on the underside of the leaves but may also appear on the top of them. Rust may cause witches broom growth (redish, twisted stems and leaves), cankers, oozes, and spotted needles. Rust may cause leaves to fall prematurely. If enough of the leaves fall, the plant may die. Plants may be stunted, off color, and weak.

diseases.name: citrus black spot disease
symptoms: No information

diseases.name: cowpea gray mold
symptoms: Water-soaked lesions on leaves, stems, pods, and flowers that later turn brown or black.
Blighting of leaves and flowers that may result in defoliation and flower abortion.
Soft rotting of pods and seeds that may cause seed discoloration and shriveling.
Reduced plant growth, vigor, yield, and quality.

diseases.name: cowpea mosaic virus
symptoms: According to and , the symptoms of cowpea mosaic virus infection vary depending on the strain of the virus, the host plant, the environmental conditions and the stage of plant growth. The most common symptoms are mosaic patterns of light and dark green on the leaves, which may also show blistering, distortion, curling or necrosis (Photo 1). The stems may show streaks or ringspots of yellow or brown. The pods may show mottling or deformation. The seeds may show discoloration or malformation. The plant growth may be stunted and the yield may be reduced.

diseases.name: cowpea red spot disease
symptoms: According to , cowpea scab disease causes small white spots on leaves, broken leaf margins, egg-shaped white spots surrounded by red or brown ring on stems, and pit-like spots with grey powder surrounded by brown borders on pods. The disease affects all plant parts above the soil causing up to 100% yield loss.

According to and , Cercospora leaf spot disease causes various sized spots often yellowish in color or with a yellow halo, others brown to purplish; these normally develop first on lower leaves. With Cercospora leafspot a dark, moldy growth develops on the lower leaf surface opposite the spot. Leafspot diseases are most serious during periods of prolonged moist weather and on late summer or early fall plantings. Severe leaf spotting results in defoliation with subsequent yield reductions.

diseases.name: cowpea rust
symptoms: According to and , the symptoms of cowpea rust disease are small reddish-brown pustules on both surfaces of leaves, petioles and stems. The pustules may coalesce to form large necrotic areas on the leaves. The infected leaves may turn yellow and fall prematurely. The disease interferes with normal root development and uptake of nutrients by plant roots resulting in reduced seed size and considerable yield loss.

diseases.name: cowpea shrink virus disease
symptoms: According to , the symptoms of cowpea curly top virus usually begin with stunting and puckering on the leaves. The name curly top comes from the symptoms the infection causes in the leaves of the plant: twisting, curling, and rolling. The branches also become distorted. They bend downward, while the leaves curl up. The plant may also show yellowing or reddening of the veins and interveinal areas, and reduced pod and seed production.

diseases.name: cowpea sooty blotch
symptoms: According to , some common symptoms of fungal diseases of cowpea are: tan to brown sunken lesions on leaves; lesions merging to girdle stems and petioles; lesions may become covered in pink spore masses during periods of wet weather (ashy stem blight); tan to brown angular leaf spots with yellow margins on leaves, pods and stems (bacterial blight); irregular light and dark green mosaic patterns in the leaves; thickened, malformed leaves (mosaic); marginal yellowing and burning of leaves (physiological); watersoaked pods and stalks supporting the pods; whiskery-type fungal growth on the affected tissue (pod blight); light, grayish, powdery growth on the leaves, pods and stems (powdery mildew).
According to , some symptoms of cowpea mosaic virus are: mosaic pattern, leaf distortion, blisters and reduction in size of leaves, stunted growth, and dark green appearance of plants.

diseases.name: cowpea spot blight
symptoms: On leaves: Water-soaked, often angular shaped spots on leaves that gradually grow to form large-brown spots of dead tissue, often surrounded by a very narrow zone of yellow tissue. Spots can form at the margins and interveinal regions. In severe infections, the plant appears burnt and dead. Leaves remain attached.

On pods: Spots are generally circular, slightly sunken, water-soaked and dark green in color. As spots age they turn dark red-brown in color and under extremely humid conditions are covered with bacterial ooze.
On stems: Water-soaked spots turn red-brown in color, generally without a yellow margin. Stems may be girdled, leading to wilting. Stem spots are uncommon.
On seed: Yellow or brown-colored spots on seed. Seed may be shrivelled and show poor germination.

diseases.name: cucumber anthracnose
symptoms: The disease initially appears as small, circular, water-soaked spots on the leaves, petioles, stems, and fruits of the plant .
The spots enlarge and become sunken, brown to black lesions with tan to gray centers .
The lesions may have concentric rings or a target-like appearance, giving the disease its name .
The infected leaves may turn yellow, dry up, and fall off prematurely, resulting in defoliation and reduced photosynthesis .
The infected fruits may develop cracks or rotting spots that render them unmarketable.

diseases.name: cucumber bacterial angular spot disease
symptoms: Small, circular, water-soaked spots on the leaves that expand into large, angular to irregular, brown or straw-colored spots with a yellow halo . The spots are confined by the leaf veins, giving them an angular appearance .
Leaf spots dry and drop out, leaving irregularly shaped holes in the leaves .
Water-soaked tan, small circular spots on fruit that may develop into bacterial soft rot and destroy the entire fruit .
Changes in color, shape or function of other plant parts such as stems and vines.

diseases.name: cucumber black spot
symptoms: Small yellow or brown water-soaked spots on the leaves that grow and darken into sunken lesions .
Coalescence of spots that cause leaf blight, stem cankers, and fruit rot .
Characteristic black, sunken lesions on affected fruit.

diseases.name: cucumber crinkle virus disease
symptoms: The most common symptom of this disease is a mosaic pattern of yellow and green patches on the leaves of infected plants . The leaves may also curl downwards and become distorted and wrinkled .
The growth of infected plants may be stunted due to a shortening of the stem segments between leaves (internodes) . The plants may also produce fewer flowers and fruits .
The fruits of infected plants may be deformed, bumpy, pale, grayish, or bitter-tasting . In flowers, white streaks or breaks may appear on the petals.

diseases.name: cucumber downy mildew
symptoms: Angular yellow spots on the upper surface of leaves that may coalesce and turn brown as the disease progresses .
Chlorosis and wilting of leaves, leading to premature defoliation and reduced yield and quality of fruits .
No direct effect on fruits, but indirect effects such as sunscald, poor flavor, misshapen cucumbers, and poor storability may occur due to loss of foliage .

diseases.name: cucumber powdery mildew
symptoms: Pale yellow leaf spots that appear on the upper or lower leaf surfaces.
White powdery spots or blotches that form on the leaves, stems and petioles and quickly expand to cover the entire foliage .
Premature senescence of infected leaves that results in reduced photosynthesis and plant vigor.
Reduced fruit yield and quality due to decreased size or number of fruit, sunburnt fruit, premature or incomplete ripening, poor storability and poor flavor .
Speckling or imperfections on fruit rind due to plant stress caused by powdery mildew infections.

diseases.name: cucumber target spot disease
symptoms: Angular, yellow spots on older leaves that enlarge and become circular with light brown centers and dark brown borders .
Coalescence of spots that cause leaf drop, stem lesions, and fruit rot .
Early infection at the blossom end that results in darkened, shriveled fruit.
Root and flower infection that can also occur.

diseases.name: cucumber white spot disease
symptoms: The main symptom of powdery mildew is the appearance of white, powdery spots on the upper surface of the leaves, stems, and sometimes fruits. The spots may coalesce and cover large areas of the plant tissue.
The infected leaves may become yellow, brown, or necrotic, and eventually fall off. The infected fruits may become misshapen, discolored, or rotten.
The disease may also reduce the plant vigor, growth, yield, and quality.

diseases.name: ginger anthracnose
symptoms: Small, circular, brown to black spots or lesions on the leaves and stems, which may coalesce and cause blighting or defoliation .
Sunken, dark brown lesions on the rhizomes that can affect the quality and yield of the crop .
Brown spots or blight on the flowers .

diseases.name: ginger bacterial leaf spot disease
symptoms: Water-soaked spots appear at the collar region of the pseudo stem and progress both side upwards and downwards.
Mild drooping and curling of leaf margins of the lower leaves which spread upwards.
Yellowing starts from the lowermost leaves and gradually progresses to the upper leaves.
Severe yellowing and wilting symptoms occur in the advanced stage.
The vascular tissues of the affected pseudo stems show dark streaks.
The affected pseudo stem and rhizome extrude milky ooze from the vascular strands when pressed gently.
Rhizomes get rotted and give off a foul smell due to rotting matter .

diseases.name: ginger leaf curl disease
symptoms: The green leaves start to curl and roll under due to water stress caused by the bacteria that block the vascular system of the plant. The lower leaves are affected first, followed by the upper leaves .
The leaves of infected plants turn yellow and then brown within 3 to 5 days as the disease progresses. The leaves eventually dry up and fall off .
The stems of infected plants become soft and rotted, breaking off from the rhizome. The stems may also show a water-soaked appearance and ooze a slimy substance when cut .
The rhizomes of infected plants show a grayish-brown discoloration and a waterlogged texture. The rhizomes may also rot completely and give off a foul smell .

diseases.name: ginger mosaic virus disease
symptoms: Mosaic or mottled pattern of light and dark green patches on the leaves .
Chlorosis or yellowing of the leaves along the veins or margins .
Necrotic spots or streaks on the leaves or stems .
Stunting or dwarfing of the plants and reduced rhizome yield .

diseases.name: ginger phytotoxicity
symptoms: Chlorosis or yellowing of the leaves and stems .
Necrosis or browning of the leaf margins and tips .
Curling or wilting of the leaves and stems .
Stunting or dwarfing of the plants and reduced rhizome yield .

diseases.name: ginger white spot disease
symptoms: Root rot: wilting, yellowing, stunting, or death of plants; decayed or discolored roots; reduced root growth; poor flowering.
Stem canker: sunken or swollen lesions on stems; cracking or splitting of bark; girdling or dieback of branches; oozing of gum or resin; reduced flowering or fruiting.
Leaf spot: circular or irregular spots or blotches on leaves; spots may have different colors or margins depending on the pathogen; premature leaf drop; reduced photosynthesis and growth.
Rust: orange or brown pustules or spores on leaves or stems; yellowing or browning of infected tissues; defoliation; reduced vigor and quality.
Powdery mildew: white or gray powdery growth on leaves or stems; distortion or curling of leaves; reduced flowering or fruiting; susceptibility to secondary infections.
Botrytis blight: grayish-brown fuzzy growth on flowers, buds, leaves, or stems; wilting or browning of infected tissues; soft rotting or collapse of flowers.

diseases.name: grape black measles
symptoms: Interveinal striping on leaves that start out as dark red in red cultivars and yellow in white cultivars, and then dry and become necrotic .
Small, round, dark spots on berries that are each bordered by a brown-purple ring .
Sudden dieback of the entire shoot or adjacent shoots in a severe form of the disease known as "apoplexy".

diseases.name: grape black rot
symptoms: Brown circular lesions on leaves, shoots, tendrils, and stems. The lesions may have a black border and a tan center with tiny black dots (pycnidia).
The lesions may coalesce and cause large areas of dead tissue on the plant tissues. On fruits, symptoms appear as small brown spots that enlarge and turn black.
The infected fruits shrivel and dry up into hard black mummies that remain attached to the cluster .

diseases.name: grape fan leaf virus disease
symptoms: Stunted or reduced vigor of the vines.
Severe distortion, asymmetry, cupping, puckering, and acute dentations of the leaves.
Yellow mottling, spots, rings, lines, or veinal and interveinal areas on the leaves.
Abnormal branching, double nodes, short or different length internodes, fasciations, and zigzag growth of the canes.
Reduced size and number of clusters with irregular ripening and shot berries.
Uneven size and seedlessness of berries with decreased sugar content and acidity.

diseases.name: grape herbicide phytotoxicity
symptoms: Growth regulator herbicides: distorted leaves, cupped leaves, downward bending of shoots, yellowing of leaves, fingering of leaf margins, reduced fruit size and quality, delayed fruit ripening.
Non-selective herbicides: chlorosis (yellowing) of leaves, necrosis (browning) of leaves, wilting of shoots, stunting of growth, reduced yield and quality.
Amino acid synthesis inhibitors (such as imazapyr or sulfometuron): chlorosis of leaves, reddening of veins, necrosis of leaf tips and margins, reduced growth and vigor.
Photosynthesis inhibitors (such as atrazine or metribuzin): chlorosis of leaves, necrosis of leaf veins and interveinal areas, bleaching of leaves.

diseases.name: grape leaf blight
symptoms: bright yellow spots on the upper surface of leaves that turn brown and necrotic with time .
white fluffy growth on the lower surface of the spots that contain spores .
premature leaf drop in severe cases .
The disease can also affect shoots, tendrils, inflorescences, and berries, causing brown lesions, wilting, and rotting .

diseases.name: grape leaf roll virus disease
symptoms: Leaf tissue between the veins turns deep red to purple in red-skinned varieties, or yellow in white-skinned varieties, with downward curling or cupping of the leaf margins. The veins remain green.
Reduced vigor and growth of the vines, and increased susceptibility to winter injury due to reduced carbohydrate stores.
Yield losses of 30-50%, delayed and uneven ripening of fruit, reduction in brix and berry color, and increase in titratable acids.
Poor quality and taste of the grapes due to disrupted balance between acidity and sugar.

diseases.name: grapes anthracnose
symptoms: Reddish brown and circular to angular spots on the upper surface of the leaves starting in late spring. As spots merge, they form irregular, reddish brown blotches. The center of the leaf spot turns tannish brown and is surrounded by a black margin. Black, speck-sized fruiting bodies (pycnidia) are arranged in a definite ring just inside the margin of the lesion .

Small spots on the green berries that become circular and whitish tan, often surrounded by a brown ring. The spots grow rapidly and may cover half of the berry within 48 hours. Within a few days the entire berry becomes coal black, hard, and mummified. The surface of the withered fruit is soon covered with minute, black, pimple-like pycnidia that are arranged in circular zones .

diseases.name: hawthorn leaf spot disease
symptoms: Numerous small red-purple spots develop on infected leaves, green twigs and fruit. Several weeks after infection the spots often coalesce into purplish-gray-brown blotches.
By mid-summer the leaves turn prematurely yellow and drop from severely infected trees.
Green fruit is deformed by some types of rust fungi that infect hawthorns.

diseases.name: hawthorn rust
symptoms: Hawthorn rust causes small yellow spots to appear on the leaves of hawthorns after infection in spring. As the spots mature and enlarge, they take on an orange color and develop tiny black dots (spermagonia) in the center of the lesion.

By mid-summer, tubes (aecia) are visible on the undersides of mature leaf lesions or within the lesions on fruit, petioles, or twigs infections. Hawthorn rust aecia are longer than the aecia of cedar-apple rust.
With severe rust, hawthorn foliage may turn bright yellow and drop prematurely. In addition, fruit and young shoots may become infected.

The symptoms vary when other hosts are infected. On quince, black flat lesions (without aecia) form on fruit. Dark brown to blackish spots with reddish halos form on the upper surface of pear leaves. In addition, aecia may develop on the petioles and lower surface of pear leaves.

diseases.name: jamun leaf spot
symptoms: On leaves: Scattered spots that are light brown or reddish brown in color. The spots may coalesce and form irregular lesions that cause leaf distortion and defoliation .
On fruits: Small sunken spots that enlarge and become dark brown or black. The spots may crack and ooze pinkish spore masses. The affected fruits rot and shrivel .

diseases.name: jujube rust
symptoms: small irregular reddish brown pustules on the upper surface of the leaves, which later coalesce and cover the entire leaf surface. The infected leaves become dry and fall off prematurely. The fruits may also develop pustules and become deformed or cracked .

diseases.name: kiwifruit brown spot disease
symptoms: Small, dark brown leaf spots that enlarge irregularly and develop into brown necrotic spots with dying curly edges .
Premature defoliation and fruit drop that reduce fruit yield and quality .
Soft and shriveled fruits with gray mold that appear mostly at the stem end.

diseases.name: kiwifruit gray spot
symptoms: Mature kiwi fruit becoming soft and shriveled with a gray growth that appears mostly at the stem end .
Affected areas appearing darker than the healthy part of the fruit, and the discoloration advancing evenly toward the base of the fruit.
Internally, infected tissue turning dark green and water soaked.

diseases.name: kiwifruit leaf spot disease
symptoms: Phoma sp.: Circular to irregular brown spots with dark margins on leaves.
Fusarium fujikuroi: Brown spots with yellow halos on leaves.
Phomopsis sp.: Brown spots with gray centers and dark margins on leaves.

diseases.name: kiwifruit mosaic virus disease
symptoms: This disease are mainly characterized by mosaic patterns on the leaves, which are mottled with yellow, white, and light or dark green spots and streaks .
The leaves may also be wrinkled, curled, or smaller than normal .
The plants may be stunted or grow poorly .
The fruits may be mottled and develop raised “warty” areas.

diseases.name: leek fertilizer damage
symptoms: Leek fertilizer damage can cause various symptoms depending on the type and amount of fertilizer applied, the soil condition, and the environmental factors. Some common symptoms are:

Leaf burn: This is caused by excess salts in the soil or on the leaf surface due to over-fertilization or evaporation. The leaves may turn brown, yellow, or white at the tips or edges and become dry and brittle.
Root rot: This is caused by fungal or bacterial infections due to poor drainage or overwatering. The roots may turn brown, black, or soft and decayed. The plants may wilt, yellow, or die.

Bolting: This is caused by stress due to high temperature, drought, or over-fertilization. The plants may produce flower stalks prematurely and stop growing leaves. The bulbs may become small, hard, or woody.

diseases.name: leek gray mold
symptoms: Grayish colored soft, mushy spots on leaves, stems, flowers and produce .
Spots may become covered with a coating of gray fungus spores, especially if humidity is high .
Fruit or plants shrivel and rot and often develop black, stone-like sclerotia under rotted parts .
When inner petals or the middle of the petals develop brown lesions first, gray mold is probably the reason.
On leaves that are relatively thin such as redbud, lesions are light brown to brown and irregular.
On fleshier leaves such as rhododendrons concentric rings of different shades of brown may be visible.

diseases.name: leek hail damage
symptoms: Bruising: Hailstones can cause bruises or lesions on the leaves or stems of leek plants, which may appear as dark green, brown, or black spots or streaks. Bruising can reduce the photosynthetic capacity and marketability of leek plants.

Shredding: Hailstones can also cause shredding or tearing of the leaves or stems of leek plants, which may expose the inner tissues to air and water loss. Shredding can also increase the susceptibility of leek plants to secondary infections by pathogens or pests.

Breaking: Hailstones can also cause breaking or snapping of the leaves or stems of leek plants, which may result in complete loss of plant parts or reduced plant growth. Breaking can also affect the quality and yield of leek plants.

diseases.name: lemon citrus canker
symptoms: Small yellow spots on the upper surface of leaves, which later turn brown and necrotic . Orange to brown pustules on the lower surface of leaves, which produce urediniospores . Raised lesions with a crater-like appearance and concentric circles on fruits, twigs, and leaves . Water-soaked margin and yellow halo around the lesions . Dieback, leaf loss, and fruit dropping in severe cases .

diseases.name: lentil leaf spot disease
symptoms: The disease symptoms of lentil spot disease are spot-like lesions that appear on all above-ground parts of the plant, including leaves, stems, pods, and seeds . The lesions are initially light gray, but become tan with a dark brown margin. The centers of the lesions become speckled with tiny black dots, which are the fruiting bodies of the fungus. The lesions can coalesce and cause defoliation, pod abortion, seed discoloration, and reduced yield and quality.

diseases.name: lentil virus disease
symptoms: Chlorosis: Yellowing of leaves or veins caused by some viruses, such as AMV, BLRV, BWYV, CMV, etc . Mosaic or mottling: Irregular patterns of green and yellow on leaves caused by some viruses, such as AMV, BYMV, CMV, etc . Stunting: Reduced plant growth caused by some viruses, such as AMV, BLRV, BWYV, BYMV, CMV, etc . Leaf rolling or curling: Upward or downward bending of leaf margins caused by some viruses, such as BLRV and BYMV . Necrosis: Browning or dying of leaf tissue caused by some viruses, such as AMV and BYMV .
Reddening: Purple or red coloration of leaves or stems caused by some viruses, such as BLRV and BWYV .
Malformation: Abnormal shape or size of leaves or flowers caused by some viruses, such as AMV and BYMV .
Reduced flowering and pod formation: Decreased number or size of flowers or pods caused by some viruses, such as AMV, BLRV, BWYV, BYMV, CMV etc .
Small and shriveled seeds: Reduced quality or quantity of seeds caused by some viruses, such as AMV and BYMV .

diseases.name: lentils brown spot
symptoms: Small, angular, pale green, water-soaked spots on the leaves
Large brown blotches with dry centers (commonly surrounded by a narrow yellow zone) on the leaves
Scorched and withered leaves that may drop off
Reduced growth, yield and quality of the plant

diseases.name: lentils white spot disease
symptoms: Leucoplakia causes white spots or patches on the leaves and pods of Lablab purpureus. The spots are irregular in shape and size, and may coalesce to form large necrotic areas. The spots may also have a yellow halo around them. The infected leaves may curl, distort or drop prematurely. The infected pods may become shriveled, discolored or deformed.

diseases.name: mango anthracnose
symptoms: On leaves: Small brown to black spots or lesions appear on young leaves, which may coalesce and cause leaf distortion or defoliation .
On flowers: Brown to black spots or blights appear on flower panicles, which may cause flower abortion or failure of fruit set .

On fruits: Dark brown to black spots or lesions appear on young fruits, which may enlarge and form sunken areas with pinkish spore masses in wet conditions . On mature fruits, the lesions may cause dark blemishes or cracks on the fruit skin, affecting the appearance and quality of the fruits .

diseases.name: melon downy mildew
