symptoms: Yellow to pale green spots on the upper side of the leaves that are restricted by leaf veins. The spots later turn brown and necrotic .
Dark purplish-grey fuzz on the underside of the leaves in high humidity. This is the sporulation of the pathogen .
Rapid defoliation of the plants when the disease is severe. The leaves appear as if frost had killed them .
Exaggerated upward leaf curling on watermelons .

diseases.name: melon powdery mildew
symptoms: Small, circular, yellow lesions on leaves and petioles that may coalesce and cause chlorosis.
White, powdery spots that may form on both surfaces of leaves, on shoots, and sometimes on flowers and fruit. These spots gradually spread over a large area of the leaves and stems.
Premature senescence of infected leaves that may result in reduced market quality because fruit become sunburnt or ripen prematurely or incompletely.
Stress from disease that can lead to imperfections on fruit rind such as speckling, raised indentations, and oedema.

diseases.name: mung bean brown spot disease
symptoms: Small water-soaked spots that develop into distinctive necrotic brown spots about 3-8mm in diameter, often with a narrow, diffuse yellow margin on leaves and stems .
The spots may enlarge, coalesce and fall out giving the leaves a tattered appearance .
Sunken brown spots can form on the pods .
If infection occurs early in pod development, the pod may become bent or twisted at the infection site .

diseases.name: mung bean magnesium deficiency
symptoms: Interveinal chlorosis, or yellowing between the leaf veins, which may turn reddish or brown in some cases.
Leaf margin curling*, either upward or downward, depending on the leaf age and environmental conditions.
Stunted growth, reduced yield and poor quality of seeds and sprouts.
The disease signs of magnesium deficiency in mung bean are:

diseases.name: mung bean sooty blotch
symptoms: Black spots or patches on the seed coat, ranging from 0.5 to 2 mm in diameter
Discoloration or darkening of the seed coat, ranging from brown to black
Rotting or shriveling of the seed embryo or cotyledons
Reduced seed viability, germination rate, and yield

diseases.name: nectarine bacterial perforation disease
symptoms: Fruit spots: The infected fruit develop small brown spots that may originate from insect wounds or natural openings. The spots expand and become deep-pitted, cracked, gummed, or water-soaked. The infected areas may turn black and dry into mummies that remain attached to the tree or fall to the ground .

Leaf spots: The infected leaves develop small, water-soaked, grayish areas that become angular and purple, black, or brown in color. The spots are confined by the leaf veins and may drop out, giving the leaves a shot hole appearance. The infected leaves may also turn yellow and fall prematurely .

Twig cankers: The infected twigs develop dark, gummy lesions that are associated with leaf and flower buds. The infected buds may fail to open or die. The lesions may extend along the twig and cause branch cankers. Gummosis and fungal tufts may also appear on the infected twigs and cankers .

diseases.name: onion black spot disease
symptoms: The fungus causes black spots, streaks, patches, or sooty masses on the neck or outer scales of the onion bulb . The spots may appear in a streaking pattern along veins. The affected scales may become water-soaked and shrivel . The fungus may also invade the interior parts of the bulb and cause gray-to-black discoloration without showing external symptoms.

diseases.name: onion blight
symptoms: Botrytis leaf blight: Small white spots surrounded by green water-soaked tissue on the leaves, which later collapse and become tan colored. The spots may coalesce and cause dieback of the entire onion top.
Purple blotch: Small tan spots on the leaves, which expand rapidly and become sunken lesions with purple bands. The lesions may also coalesce and cause collapse of the entire top.

diseases.name: onion downy mildew
symptoms: Leaf yellowing and dieback from the tip downwards , resulting in reduced bulb size and quality.
Premature sprouting or shriveling of bulbs in storage.

diseases.name: onion gray mold
symptoms: Water-soaked appearance with yellow discoloration on the neck around the time of harvest.
Rot of the bulbs.
Mushy spots, brown spots, black spots, or a slimy texture on the onion.

diseases.name: onion purple spot disease
symptoms: Small, sunken, water-soaked lesions with light centers on the leaves
Lesions enlarge and turn purple to brown, often with yellow rings that create a distinctive bull鈥檚-eye pattern
Leaves turn yellow/brown and wilt, and may be girdled
Bulbs may become infected through neck wounds and show soft, water-soaked areas that turn dark reddish-purple, then brown/black .

diseases.name: onion sclerotium diseases
symptoms: The main symptom of onion microbacterium nodosum disease is bulb rot, which can occur during growth or storage . The infected bulbs become soft, watery, and foul-smelling, and may have a brownish discoloration of the inner scales . The rot may start from the neck or the base of the bulb, or from any wound or injury site .

Another symptom that may occur during growth is leaf blight, which causes yellowing, wilting, and dying of the foliage . The leaf blight may be confused with other fungal or bacterial diseases that affect onion leaves .

diseases.name: onion virus disease
symptoms: The most characteristic symptom of onion virus disease is the appearance of yellow or straw-colored lesions on the leaves, often with concentric rings or diamond shapes . These lesions can coalesce and cause premature senescence and death of the leaves .
The virus also affects the bulb development and quality, reducing the size, weight, and storability of the onions .
The virus can also cause stunting, wilting, and lodging (falling over) of the seed stalks, which reduces the seed production and viability .

diseases.name: peach bacterial spot
symptoms: Small reddish-purple spots with white centers on leaves .
Shot-hole appearance of leaves due to spot centers falling out .
Yellowing and dropping of infected leaves .
Small water-soaked markings on fruit that enlarge and merge .
Cracking or pitting of fruit along the lesions .
Brown rot infection of fruit through cracks.

diseases.name: peach iron deficiency
symptoms: Iron deficiency in peach trees are mainly related to the reduction of chlorophyll synthesis, which leads to interveinal chlorosis .
This means that the leaves turn yellow or pale green between the veins, while the veins remain green.
The chlorosis usually starts from the young leaves at the tips of the branches and spreads to the older leaves if the deficiency is severe.
In advanced stages of iron deficiency, the leaves may also show necrosis, which means that they turn brown and die.
This can result in leaf drop, twig dieback and reduced fruit quality and yield .

diseases.name: peach latent mosaic disease
symptoms: PLMVd infection vary depending on the cultivar, environmental conditions, and infection level.

Some common symptoms are leaf chlorosis, leaf curling, leaf mottling, leaf necrosis, leaf deformation, fruit deformation, fruit cracking, fruit spotting, fruit drop, reduced fruit size and quality, reduced yield, reduced vigor, and premature senescence .

diseases.name: pear tree brown spot
symptoms: On leaves: small brown spots with yellow halos that coalesce into large necrotic areas. The spots may have black dots in the center that are the pycnidia of the fungus. The infected leaves may fall prematurely .

On fruits: circular brown lesions with a sunken appearance that may crack and ooze gum. The lesions may also have black dots that are the pycnidia of the fungus. The infected fruits may drop or become mummified .
On twigs: dark brown cankers that may girdle and kill the twigs. The cankers may also have black dots that are the pycnidia of the fungus .

diseases.name: pear tree gray spot disease
symptoms: Circular, velvety, olive-black spots on leaves, fruits, and twigs that turn gray and cracked with age .
Premature leaf drop, fruit drop, or fruit deformation .
Reduced yield and quality of fruits .

diseases.name: pepper bacterial spot
symptoms: Small, water-soaked lesions with a yellow-green border on leaves .
Coalescing of spots into larger blighted areas on leaves .
Brown and dry spots with holes on leaves .
Spots and raised cracks on fruit .
Brown rot infection of fruit through cracks.

diseases.name: pepper black spot disease
symptoms: According to and , some common symptoms of black spot disease of pepper caused by fungal pathogens are: Brown or black spots or lesions on the fruits, which may become sunken or cracked Pink spores or fungal growths on the spots or lesions Soft rot or decay of the infected fruitsBlack growths or blights on the leaves and stems

According to , some common symptoms of black spot disease of pepper caused by bacterial pathogens are: Clear raised bumps or blisters on the fruits, which may turn black as the disease progressesDistortion or twisting of the leaves and fruitsYellowing or browning of the leaf marginsLeaf drop or wilting

diseases.name: pepper blight
symptoms: Dark brown lesions on stems near the soil line that girdle and kill the plant
Sudden wilting and collapse of plants
Dark green, circular or irregular spots on leaves that dry to a light tan color
Blackened and shriveled fruits with raised, corky spots

diseases.name: pepper brown spot disease
symptoms: Yellow and green spots on leaves
Brown spots on leaves in later stages
Raised, corky spots on peppers
Leaf drop and decaying peppers
Brown or black spots at the tip or underside of the peppers
Soft and sunken areas on the peppers
These symptoms may be caused by different factors such as bacterial leaf spot, sunscald, phytophthora blight, blossom end rot, calcium deficiency, or anthracnose .

diseases.name: pepper cladosporium grease(leaf)mold
symptoms: According to , pepper cladosporium grease(leaf)mold can cause different symptoms depending on the part of the plant infected and the stage of growth. Some common symptoms are:
Dark brown lesions near the soil line that girdle the stem and cause wilting and death of the plant.
Dark green circular or irregular spots on the leaves that dry to a light tan color.
Raised corky spots on the fruits that blacken and shrivel.

diseases.name: pepper leaf blight
symptoms: According to and , the symptoms of pepper leaf blight disease are small yellow and green spots on leaves that turn brown and necrotic. The spots may coalesce to form large blighted areas on the leaves. The infected leaves may fall prematurely. The disease may also affect stems and fruits, causing sunken lesions or rotting.

diseases.name: pepper powdery mildew
symptoms: The disease symptoms of powdery mildew of pepper are :Powdery-white fungal growth on the undersides of leaves. Light-green to yellow blotches on the upper leaf surfaces. Brown necrotic spots or patches on the leaves. Leaf curling, wilting and dropping. Reduced fruit size and quality.

diseases.name: pepper umbilical rot
symptoms: The disease symptoms of umbilical rot of pepper are : Sunken, brownish-black lesions on the blossom end of the fruits, Softening and collapsing of the affected tissues, Premature ripening and dropping of the fruits, Secondary infections by soft-rot bacteria or fungi.

diseases.name: pepper virus disease
symptoms: CMV: The symptoms of CMV infection vary depending on the strain of the virus, the age of the plant, and the environmental conditions. Some common symptoms are ring-spot or oak-leaf necrotic patterns on lower leaves (fig. 1), mosaic or mottling on upper leaves (fig. 3), stunting, distortion, and malformation of leaves, flowers, and fruits (fig. 2), and reduced yield and quality.
TMV: The symptoms of TMV infection include mosaic or mottling on leaves, stunting, leaf curling or rolling, necrotic spots on stems and petioles, and fruit deformation.
TEV: The symptoms of TEV infection include mosaic or mottling on leaves, leaf curling or crinkling, vein clearing or banding, stunting, flower abortion or sterility, and fruit deformation.
TSWV: The symptoms of TSWV infection include necrotic spots or rings on leaves, stems, and fruits (fig. 4), mosaic or mottling on leaves, stunting, wilting, and reduced yield and quality.
AMY: The symptoms of AMY infection include mosaic or mottling on leaves, yellowing or chlorosis on leaf margins or veins (fig. 5), stunting, flower abortion or sterility, and fruit deformation.
PMMoV: The symptoms of PMMoV infection include mild mosaic or mottling on leaves (fig. 6), stunting, reduced yield and quality.

diseases.name: pepper white spot disease
symptoms: Powdery mildew: The symptoms of powdery mildew infection include white, powdery patches on the upper or lower surface of leaves, stems, and fruits. The patches may coalesce and cover large areas of the plant tissue. The infected leaves may turn yellow, brown, or purple and drop off prematurely. The infected fruits may become distorted, cracked, or sunburned. The yield and quality of the fruits may be reduced .

diseases.name: pumpkin downy mildew
symptoms: Yellow spots on the upper leaf surface between the leaf veins. These spots spread everywhere but the veins and eventually turn brown.
The plant cannot photosynthesize on these yellow or brown spots.
When the leaf is totally brown, it drops.
The exposed pumpkins can be scalded by the sun, ripen poorly, and grow to be much smaller than expected.
Flower buds can also be infected which will prevent them from opening and they'll dry and fall off the plant.

diseases.name: pumpkin powdery mildew
symptoms: White to gray powdery spots or patches on the leaves and stems, especially on the lower surface and older leaves .
Distortion, curling, yellowing, browning, and dieback of infected leaves and stems .
Reduced plant growth, vigor, flowering, and fruiting .
Sunburn or scalding of exposed fruits due to loss of foliage cover .

diseases.name: pumpkin virus disease
symptoms: Mosaic pattern on leaves, which may appear as yellow or green mottling, blistering, distortion, or curling . Stunted growth and reduced yield of plants .
Reduced size, shape, color, and quality of fruits . Necrotic spots or rings on fruits in some cases.

diseases.name: radish black rot disease
symptoms: Seedlings may die prematurely or become yellow and drop lower leaves.
Older plants may develop yellowing at leaf margins in a wedge or V-shape, followed by browning and necrosis . Affected leaves may drop off.
Roots may develop dark spots, cracks, and lesions that turn black and dry . Root growth may be distorted or constricted.
Cauliflower curds may become infected and turn brown.

diseases.name: radish black spot disease
symptoms: On leaves: areas that look water-soaked, small spots that are tan or white in color, yellowing at leaf margins in a wedge shape, veins turning black, distortion and withering of leaves .
On petioles: black, sunken spots that are elongated.
On roots: dark patches, cracks and fissures that turn necrotic, rotten roots.

diseases.name: radish fertilizer damage
symptoms: Fertilizer damage to radish are mainly changes in color and shape of the leaves and roots.
Too much fertilizer can cause leaf burn, which is characterized by yellowing or browning of the leaf margins or tips, curling or wilting of the leaves, and stunted growth.
Too little fertilizer can cause nutrient deficiency, which is characterized by pale green or yellow leaves, purple veins, small or misshapen roots, and reduced yield.

diseases.name: radish mosaic virus disease
symptoms: Mosaic: The leaves show yellow, white, or light and dark green spots and streaks that give them a mosaic-like appearance .
Ringspots: The leaves may also develop circular or irregular spots with chlorotic or necrotic centers and green margins.
Leaf distortion: The leaves may become wrinkled, curled, or small due to the virus infection .
Veinal necrosis: The veins of the leaves may turn brown or black and die due to the virus infection.
Systemic necrosis: The whole plant may wilt and die due to severe virus infection.

diseases.name: radish shrink virus disease
symptoms: This disease is interveinal chlorosis, yellowing, and drying of lower leaves on radish plants.
The symptoms are more severe at high temperature and low humidity conditions.

diseases.name: radish white spot disease
symptoms: White, raised spore masses on the underside of the leaves, which may be barely visible to a ½ inch (1 cm) across or larger .
Yellowing, wilting, curling, or distortion of the leaves, which may eventually fall off prematurely .
Reduced plant growth, vigor, yield, and quality .
Infection of other plant parts such as stems, flowers, and pods, which may show white spots or lesions .

diseases.name: radish yellow leaf spot disease
symptoms: Small, circular, gray to brown spots with dark margins on the leaves
Enlarged and coalesced spots that cause the leaves to turn yellow and wilt
Long lesions of greenish-gray on the petioles and stems
Reduced root size and quality
Sunscald on exposed roots

diseases.name: spinach black spot disease
symptoms: Small, circular to irregular spots on the leaves that are initially yellowish-green or brownish-red .
As the spots mature, they become olive-black and may coalesce to form large necrotic areas .
The spots may have a concentric ring pattern or a dark margin .
The infected leaves may curl, dry up, and fall off .
The ripening seed on spinach seed crops may also develop lesions, shrivel, and become infected with the fungus.

diseases.name: strawberry brown spot disease
symptoms: Leaf spot: Purple or gray patches on the leaves that turn brown and dry out. The patches may have light-colored centers or reddish-purple borders. The leaves may curl, wilt or drop off .
Leather rot: Brown spots on the fruits that make them tough and inedible. The spots may have a water-soaked appearance or a white halo. The fruits may also have a bitter or sour taste .
Anthracnose fruit rot: Brown circular spots on the fruits that decompose them. The spots may have salmon-colored spores on them. The fungus may also infect the crown, stems and leaves of the plant .

diseases.name: strawberry calcium deficiency
symptoms: Younger leaves develop tip burn and as leaves expand they become cupped and distorted.
Marginal leaf yellowing can also occur with more severe deficiencies.
Petioles can develop darken lesions.
Fruit are smaller and have a hard texture.
Root growth is also less.

diseases.name: strawberry leaf blight
symptoms: Reddish purple spots 3 ⁄ 8 ” to 1 ⁄ 2 ” in diameter that develop on leaflets and eventually enlarge to become V-shaped and delineated by major leaf veins . The enlarged lesions develop a dark brown central zone surrounded by a lighter brown zone. An outer zone of purple, red or yellow can also develop.
Entire stems may be killed when petioles and trusses become completely girdled with black to purple, elongate, and sunken lesions.
Fruit lesions can be circular and sunken, with a dark brown to black color. They may be confused with anthracnose fruit rot.
Severe defoliation may occur in nursery fields or perennial plantings, resulting in reduced yields and plant quality .

diseases.name: strawberry leaf scorch
symptoms: Small, irregular-shaped purplish-brown spots on the upper leaf surface that enlarge and darken over time .
Coalescence of spots and purplish to bright red coloration of the tissue between the lesions, depending on cultivar, temperature, or other factors.
Browning, drying, and curling of the leaf margins, giving the leaf a scorched appearance .
Elongate, sunken, purplish to brown lesions on the petioles that can girdle and kill the leaf.
Infection of runners, fruit stalks, fruit and caps that can cause wilting and death of flowers and fruit .
Reduction in yield, plant vigor, and crown number due to weakened plants that are more susceptible to winter and drought stress .

diseases.name: strawberry white leaf spot
symptoms: On leaves: Small, circular, water-soaked spots that enlarge and become brown or purple with gray or tan centers. The spots may coalesce and cause leaf blight or defoliation .
On stems: Dark brown to black lesions that girdle the stem and cause wilting or dieback of the plant .
On fruits: Small, sunken, brown spots that expand and become dark purple or black with orange spore masses. The infected fruits may become hard, dry, and mummified .

diseases.name: tomato bacterial spot
symptoms: On leaves: Small (less than â…› inch), circular, water-soaked spots that may be yellow-green at first but turn brownish-red as they age. The spots may coalesce and cause leaf yellowing, blighting and defoliation.
On stems: Brown, elongated lesions that may girdle and kill stems.

On fruits: Small, raised, blister-like spots that may have a yellow halo on green fruits. The spots become larger (up to Â¼ inch), brown, scabby and rough on mature fruits. The spots may be raised or sunken with raised edges.

diseases.name: tomato early blight
symptoms: The most common symptom of early blight in both seedlings and mature tomato plants is small-ish brown spots developing on the lower leaves. The spots typically have concentric rings inside them that give a target or bullseye appearance, and are often surrounded with a light green or yellow halo.
As the disease progresses, the spots enlarge and coalesce, causing the leaves to turn yellow and wither. The disease usually starts from the bottom of the plant and moves upward.
The fruits may also develop dark, leathery spots with raised ridges and concentric rings at the stem end. Both green and ripe fruits can be affected.

diseases.name: tomato gray leaf spot
symptoms: Brown to black specks on older leaves, then newer growth
Gray center surrounded by a yellow halo on the spots
Leaves turn yellow, then brown, then drop
Center of the spot may fall out leaving holes
Entire plant may be affected and stunted
Fruit is not affected directly, but may suffer from sunscald due to defoliation

diseases.name: tomato late blight
symptoms: Irregular to circular lesions on leaves, often with a lighter halo or ring around them. These lesions may appear water-soaked or dry out depending on the weather conditions .
White cottony growth on the underside of leaves and stems, indicating the presence of spores and mycelia of the fungus .
Browning, shriveling and dying of leaves and stems as the disease progresses .
Firm, dark, greasy-looking lesions on tomato fruits, which may become leathery and chocolate brown in color. These lesions can cover the entire fruit and cause it to rot .

diseases.name: tomato leaf mold
symptoms: The disease symptoms of tomato leaf mold are mainly visible on the leaves of the plant . They include: Pale green to yellow spots on the upper side of the leaves, usually less than 1/4 inch in diameter, with no definite margins; Olive-green to brown velvety mold on the lower side of the leaves, below the spots; Leaf spots coalescing and turning brown as the disease progresses; Leaf curling, withering, and dying, but often remaining attached to the plant; In severe cases, the disease symptoms may also affect the flowers and fruits of the plant . They include: Infected flowers turning black and falling off; Fruit infections starting as a smooth black irregular area on the stem end of the fruit; Infected fruit becoming sunken, dry, and leathery.

diseases.name: tomato leaf spot disease
symptoms: Brown to black round spots with dark borders and beige centers on the lower leaves, moving up to the upper leaves as the disease progresses .
Yellowing, wilting, curling, or dropping of the infected leaves .
Dark spots with concentric rings on the stems or fruits .
Reduced plant growth and fruit yield .

diseases.name: tomato septoria leaf spot
symptoms: Circular, tan to gray lesions on the lower leaves and stems of the plant. The lesions are typically small (\.
The lesions generally have a dark-brown perimeter and a yellow halo may develop around the leaf lesions.
The symptoms usually occur in the older, lower leaves, but the disease can develop at any stage in the plant's life. They may also appear on the petioles, calyxes, and rarely on the fruits.

As the disease progresses, spots may coalesce and the leaves may become entirely blighted. The leaves may turn yellow and eventually dry out and fall off. This will weaken the plant, send it into decline, and cause sun scalding of the unprotected fruits. Without leaves, the plant will not continue producing and maturing fruits.

diseases.name: tomato target spot disease
symptoms: Pinpoint-sized, water-soaked spots on the upper leaf surface that develop into small, necrotic lesions with light brown centers and dark margins .
Circular spots with concentric rings and a velvety black fungal lesion in the center on tomato fruit. The spots become pitted and larger as the fruit matures .
Yellow halos around the lesions on some varieties.
Lesions coalescing into large blighted areas on the leaflets and premature leaf drop .

diseases.name: walnut black spot disease
symptoms: Dark spots on the leaf blades and petioles, ranging in size from a few mm to 1.25 cm in diameter .
Leaf spots may coalesce and cause leaf yellowing, browning, and premature defoliation .
Infected nuts develop black, slightly sunken lesions at the flower end (end blight) or on the sides (side blight) .

diseases.name: zucchini powdery mildew
symptoms: White or gray powdery spots or patches on the leaves, stems, flowers, or fruits . The spots may coalesce and cover large areas of the plant surface.
Yellowing, browning, curling, wilting, or dropping of the infected leaves . The leaves may also become distorted or deformed.
Reduced growth, yield, quality, or flavor of the zucchini fruits . The fruits may also develop sunscald or rot.

diseases.name: pear tree rust
symptoms: Bright yellow to orange spots up to about 1 inch in diameter on leaves, fruits, twigs, and branches . The spots may enlarge and coalesce over time.
Tube-like projections or acorn-shaped fruiting bodies on the underside of leaves or fruits . These are brown in color and have a trellis-like appearance that gives the disease its name.
Premature leaf drop and fruit mummification in severe cases .

diseases.name: bipolaris maydis race
symptoms: The formation of small, round or oval lesions that may be yellow, brown, or even a brownish purple in color on the corn leaves.
The lesions multiply quickly and form bands across leaves. You may also see the lesions on the stalks, husks, and sheaths of your corn plants.
The lesions coalesce and cause premature leaf senescence and necrosis.
The disease reduces the photosynthetic capacity and translocation of carbohydrates to the grain, resulting in lower yield and quality.

diseases.name: cereal millet bacterial stripe disease
symptoms: The appearance of water-soaked lesions on the leaves that later become translucent and yellowish-brown . The lesions may coalesce and form streaks along the veins .
The reduction of leaf area, photosynthesis, and grain filling due to the necrosis of infected tissues .
The premature senescence and death of severely infected plants .

diseases.name: cereal millet black sheath disease
symptoms: According to one source, the symptoms of sheath blight disease include water-soaked lesions on the leaf sheaths that enlarge and coalesce to form large irregular tan or grayish-green blotches. The lesions may have a dark brown margin and may be covered with a white cottony growth of the fungus. The infected leaves may wither and die prematurely.

Another source states that the symptoms of blast disease vary depending on the plant part infected. On leaves and leaf collars, blast appears as small diamond-shaped or elliptical gray-green or gray-white spots with a brown margin. The spots may enlarge and coalesce to form large necrotic areas. On panicles, blast causes brown or black lesions on the rachis, pedicels and glumes. The infected spikelets may become sterile or discolored.

diseases.name: cereal millet leaf blight
symptoms: Small brown spots with yellow halos on the leaves, which enlarge and coalesce to form irregular lesions .
Premature defoliation of infected leaves, resulting in reduced photosynthesis and grain filling .
Reduced yield and quality of the grains due to shriveled kernels and lower test weight .

diseases.name: cereal millet powdery mildew
symptoms: White, cottony patches (colonies) of mycelium and conidia (asexual spores) on the surface of the plant. They can occur on all aerial parts of the plant, including stems and heads, but are most conspicuous on the upper surfaces of lower leaves .
The white colonies later turn dull gray-brown.
Infected leaves may become chlorotic (yellow), necrotic (brown), or distorted.
Severe infection can reduce photosynthesis, transpiration, grain filling, and yield quality and quantity .

diseases.name: corn bacterial red stripe disease
symptoms: Symptoms of bacterial leaf streak are tan, brown, or orange lesions that occur between the veins of the corn leaves. Lesions are long, narrow, and less than 1 inch to several inches long. Lesions also can occur close to the leaf midrib or across the leaf blade

diseases.name: corn bacterial stripe
symptoms: The symptoms include interveinal leaf streaks that are brown, tan, or yellow and can be short or very long. Lesions are also strikingly yellow when backlit against the sun. Lesions usually have slightly wavy edges in contrast to the smooth, linear lesion margins of gray leaf spot. Symptoms most often develop initially on the lower leaves, but can also occur in the mid-to upper canopy following storms or heavy rains.

diseases.name: corn bacterial wilt disease
symptoms: The symptoms vary depending on the type of bacterium and the growth stage of corn. Some of the most common symptoms are:

Stewart's wilt: Pale green to yellow streaks with irregular or wavy margins that parallel the leaf veins. The streaks may extend the length of the leaf and become straw-colored when they die. The lesions may also have scratch-like feeding marks of flea beetles. In seedlings, wilting and death may occur.

Goss's wilt: Elongated tan lesions with irregular margins that extend parallel to the veins. Dark, water-soaked spots (freckles) develop in the lesions. Shiny patches of dried bacterial ooze that appear similar to dry varnish are often present on the lesions. In some cases, systemic wilting and stalk rot may occur.
Bacterial stalk rot: Wilting and collapse of plants due to stalk rotting. The pith of infected stalks becomes soft and discolored, ranging from light brown to black. A foul odor may be present.

diseases.name: corn brown spot disease
symptoms: Symptoms of corn brown spot disease usually appear on mid-canopy leaves. Leaf lesions are numerous, very small (approximately ¼ inch in diameter), round to oval, yellowish to brown in color, and usually occur in broad bands across the leaf. Alternating bands of infected and non-infected tissues are common. Dark purplish to black oval spots also occur on the midrib of the leaf. Symptoms may also occur on the stalk, leaf sheath, and husks .

diseases.name: corn curvularia leaf spot
symptoms: Gray leaf spot: This disease causes rectangular or oblong lesions on the leaves that are gray to tan in color and have brown or purple margins. The lesions are usually parallel to the veins and may coalesce to form large necrotic areas. The infected leaves may wilt, dry up, or fall off prematurely .

Eyespot: This disease causes small, round to oval spots on the leaves that are light-colored and translucent with a red-brown ring and a chlorotic yellow halo. The spots are usually scattered over the leaf surface and may merge to form irregular blotches. The infected leaves may show reduced photosynthesis and yield.

diseases.name: corn dwarf mosaic virus disease
symptoms: Stippled mottle or mosaic of light and dark green on the leaves that may develop into narrow streaks on the youngest leaves.
Stunted growth and reduced tillering due to shortened internodes, resulting in a bunchy appearance of the plant.
Reduced ear formation and development, leading to low yield and quality.

diseases.name: corn genetic streak
symptoms: After the onset of the disease, the internodes of corn are shortened, the plant shape is shortened, and the leaf veins are extended to produce faded stripes. Early plant damage, growth stagnation, and premature death; Affected in the middle stage, the plant undergoes significant dwarfing, with clustered top leaves and difficult male flowers to emerge; In the later stage, the affected dwarf is not obvious and has a small impact on yield.

diseases.name: corn gray spot
symptoms: The disease first appears as small, necrotic spots with yellow halos on the lower leaves about two to three weeks before tasseling .
The spots expand to become rectangular lesions that are restricted by leaf veins and range from 0.5 to 2 inches long and 0.125 inch wide .
The lesions are gray to brown in color and have distinct parallel edges. They may appear opaque when held against the light .
The lesions may coalesce and cause large areas of leaf blighting and premature senescence. The disease may progress from the lower to the upper leaves as infection cycles occur .

diseases.name: corn northern leaf blight
symptoms: Small, circular to oval, dark brown to black spots on the leaves that resemble drops of tar . The spots may coalesce and cover large areas of the leaf surface.
Premature senescence and death of the leaves, resulting in reduced photosynthesis and grain fill.
Increased susceptibility to stalk rots and lodging due to reduced stalk strength and quality.

diseases.name: corn phosphorus deficiency
symptoms: The most common symptom of phosphorus deficiency in corn is a purple or reddish coloration on the lower leaves and stems, especially on the leaf tips and margins. This symptom is caused by the accumulation of anthocyanin pigments in the plant tissues due to reduced photosynthesis and sugar transport .

Another symptom of phosphorus deficiency in corn is stunted and spindly plants with dark green to bluish green color. The plants may also have poorly developed root systems and show signs of wilting or drought stress. Phosphorus deficiency can also delay the maturity and reduce the yield and quality of corn .

diseases.name: corn potassium deficiency
symptoms: Yellowing to necrosis (tissue death) of the outer leaf margins. These symptoms begin at the leaf tip and progress down the margin toward the leaf base .
Blue-green or bronze coloration of young emerging leaves.
Curling leaves with inter-veinal scorching and necrosis.
Narrowed and peaked cobs with poorly filled grains at the cob tips.
Woody stems that are prone to lodging.

diseases.name: corn red leaf disease
symptoms: Red stripes on the leaves that may be continuous or discontinuous, and may vary in width and length depending on the virus strain and the plant genotype.
Stunted growth and reduced tillering due to shortened internodes, resulting in a bunchy appearance of the plant.
Reduced ear formation and development, leading to low yield and quality.

diseases.name: corn root rot
symptoms: Root rot: Yellowing leaves, wilting, stunting, poor growth, and reduced yield are common symptoms of root rot diseases . The roots may be discolored, decayed, or rotted. The crown and lower stem may also show signs of rotting and discoloration . A foul odor may emanate from the soil or the infected plant parts .

Leaf spot: Small to large spots or lesions of various shapes and colors may appear on the leaves, depending on the causal agent. For example, gray leaf spot causes rectangular lesions that are restricted by the leaf veins, while common rust causes small, oval dark-reddish-brown pustules on both leaf surfaces. The spots or lesions may coalesce and cause premature leaf death or defoliation .

diseases.name: corn round spot disease
symptoms: Small, round, yellow spots on leaves and sheaths that appear in bands across the leaf. The spots turn brown and coalesce to form larger blotches. The infected leaf tissue decomposes, releasing brown spores.
Stalks often break and lodge due to weakened plant tissue.

diseases.name: corn rust
symptoms: Chlorotic flecks on the leaf surface that develop into powdery, brick-red pustules as the spores break through the leaf surface .
Rust-colored to dark brown, elongated pustules on both leaf surfaces that contain rust spores (urediniospores) and darken as they age .
Leaf chlorosis and death under severe conditions .

diseases.name: corn sheath blight
symptoms: Cigar-shaped lesions on the leaves that are tan to gray in color and range from one to six inches in length15. The lesions may coalesce and cover large areas of the leaf surface16.
Reduced photosynthesis and transpiration, resulting in stunted growth and lower yield17.
Increased susceptibility to stalk rot and ear rot pathogens18.

diseases.name: corn sooty blotch
symptoms: The disease first appears as small, circular, water-soaked spots on the lower leaves of corn plants. The spots enlarge and become brown or black with yellow halos. The spots may coalesce and form irregular patches that cover large areas of the leaf surface .
The infected leaves may curl, dry up and fall off prematurely, resulting in reduced photosynthesis and plant growth .
The disease can also infect the stalks and ears of corn plants, causing rotting, discoloration and reduced yield and quality .

diseases.name: corn sunburn
symptoms: Yellowing, browning, or whitening of the leaf tissue, especially on the upper surface and along the leaf margins .
Reduced photosynthetic capacity and transpiration rate of the affected leaves.
Increased water loss and wilting of the plants under severe stress.
Reduced plant growth, ear size, and grain yield under prolonged or repeated exposure .

diseases.name: corn top rot
symptoms: Wilting, browning, and death of the leaves and tassels in the upper part of the plant .
Stunting, yellowing, or reddening of the plant due to impaired water and nutrient transport .
Poor pollination, reduced grain fill, and ear rot due to fungal colonization of the ear tissues .
Lodging or breakage of the stalk due to weakened pith and vascular tissue .

diseases.name: corn virus disease
symptoms: Chlorotic streaks in veins of leaf that may coalesce and form large necrotic patches.
Premature aging and drying of leaves, leading to reduced photosynthesis and plant growth. Stunted plants with reduced tillering and ear formation.
Deformed ears with small or no kernels, resulting in low yield and quality.

diseases.name: corn zn deficiency
symptoms: Moderate deficiencies in seedlings may result in white to pale-yellow longitudinal areas in the newest leaves that are usually more pronounced in the lower half of the leaf. Severe deficiencies at this time may result in entire plants being pale yellow to white in color, and stunted.

Moderate deficiencies may result in interveinal chlorosis (white or yellow) in the newest growth. This chlorosis is not always uniform across the width of the leaf, but may appear as longitudinal "bands" of more chlorotic tissue. Areas of the leaf near the stalk may develop a general white to yellow discoloration.

More severe zinc deficiencies may result in bands of pale yellow to white tissue running longitudinally along both sides of the midrib, particularly in the lower half of the leaf. This tissue may eventually wilt and become necrotic. Plants may appear stunted due to shortened leaves and internodes, sometimes called "rosetting."

diseases.name: potato early blight
symptoms: Small, irregular dark brown spots on the lower or oldest leaves of the plant. The spots may enlarge and take on an angular shape with concentric rings, resembling a target. The spots may coalesce and cause the entire leaf to yellow and die .
Dark brown to black spots on the stems of the plant .

Dark gray to purple, circular to irregular lesions with raised edges on the tubers. The tuber flesh may be brown, dry, corky, or leathery. In advanced stages, the tuber flesh may look water soaked and yellow to greenish yellow.

diseases.name: potato late blight
symptoms: The symptoms of late blight can appear on leaves, stems, and tubers of potato plants.

On leaves, the symptoms are pale green, water-soaked spots that often start at the leaf tips or edges. The spots enlarge rapidly and turn dark brown to purplish black. The spots may have a bull's eye appearance with concentric rings of tissue. A white downy growth may form at the edges of the spots on the lower leaf surface .
On stems, the symptoms are dark green, brown, or black water-soaked lesions that may also have white downy growth. The lesions are firm and have a rounded edge .

On tubers, the symptoms are sunken and firm brown lesions that may extend several centimeters into the tuber. The flesh below the lesions may be brown, dry, leathery, or corky. The lesions may also become soft and rotten as secondary bacteria invade .

diseases.name: rice brown spot
symptoms: Small, circular to oval, yellow-brown or brown lesions on leaves and leaf sheaths that may coalesce and cause leaf wilting or drying .
Dark brown to black oval spots or discoloration on glumes and panicle branches that may affect grain filling and quality .
Unfilled grains or spotted or discolored seeds that may reduce seed germination and seedling vigor .

diseases.name: rice leaf blast
symptoms: Elliptical or spindle-shaped lesions on the leaves, with whitish to gray centers and brown or reddish brown borders. The lesions are usually 0.39 to 0.58 inch long and 0.12-0.2 inch wide.
Coalescence of lesions that may cover large areas of the leaf surface and cause premature defoliation of infected leaves.
Reduced growth and yield of the plant due to loss of photosynthetic area and impaired grain filling.

diseases.name: sorghum anthracnose
symptoms: Anthracnose can infect all above ground parts of the plant, including leaves, stems, peduncles and panicles .
The most common symptom is the appearance of small, oval to irregular-shaped lesions on the leaves, which are initially water-soaked and later turn tan to reddish-brown with dark brown margins .
The lesions may coalesce and cause extensive blighting of the leaves .
On the stems and peduncles, the lesions are sunken, elongated and dark brown to black, often with a reddish halo .
The infected stems and peduncles may break or lodge, resulting in reduced grain filling and yield loss .
On the panicles, the lesions are similar to those on the stems, but smaller and more scattered .
The infected grains may be shriveled, discolored or covered with pinkish fungal masses .

diseases.name: sorghum bacterial red stripe disease
symptoms: The first symptoms are narrow, water-soaked, transparent leaf streaks, 2-3 mm wide by 2-15 mm long, appearing as early as the second leaf stage of the seedling.
Lesions soon turn red, become opaque, and at intervals may broaden into somewhat irregularly shaped oval spots with tan centers and narrow red margins.
The spots may coalesce to form larger areas of infected tissue.
The leaves may become distorted, curled, or twisted.
The disease may also affect the stems, peduncles, and panicles, causing reddish-brown to black stripes or blotches that may reduce grain yield and quality .

diseases.name: sorghum bacterial stripe disease
symptoms: These stripes are confined between the veins and may have a crusty surface when the bacterial slime dries on the surface .
The stripes may coalesce and cause leaf blighting and premature defoliation.
The disease may also affect the panicles and cause reduced grain filling and quality.

diseases.name: sorghum dwarf mosaic disease
symptoms: The infected leaves show fine discontinuous chlorotic streaks between the veins that may become necrotic as the disease progresses. The infected plants become stunted in growth with short internodes. An early-infected plant may die sooner or later without emergence of earhead .
The infected leaves show reddish stripes along the veins that may turn necrotic. The infected plants show reduced tillering and delayed flowering .
The infected leaves show reddish streaks along the midrib that may extend to the leaf margins. The infected plants show reduced growth and yield .
The infected leaves show brown to black necrotic spots or patches that may cause leaf death. The infected plants show severe stunting and wilting .

diseases.name: sorghum gloeocercospora leaf
symptoms: It mainly affects leaves and leaf sheaths. The first purple-red suborbicular or oval spots, variable in size, with two to eight very obvious whorls. Usually easy to occur at the edge of the leaf, the spot into a semi-elliptic. When the weather is humid, the back surface of the spot produces slender orange-red mucilage, that is, the conidiophore and conidia of the pathogen. In severe cases, the spots converge and resemble "leopard stripes," causing the leaves to die. Many sorghum varieties do not have typical whorls on the leaves, but produce dark purple patches of varying sizes and irregular shapes that are not easy to recognize.

diseases.name: sorghum glume mold disease
symptoms: The infected leaves show elliptical to irregular brown to black spots with yellow halos that coalesce to form large necrotic areas. The spots may have grayish centers with dark margins. The infected leaves may curl, dry up, and fall off prematurely .
The infected stems show dark brown to black lesions that girdle the stem and cause wilting and death of the plant.
The infected heads show brown to black spots that enlarge and cover the entire head surface. The heads may become sterile or produce shriveled and discolored seeds .

diseases.name: sorghum mosaic disease
symptoms: The disease symptoms are mosaic patterns of contrasting shades of green on the leaves, resulting from varying levels of chlorophyll concentration in the leaf blade .
The mosaic patterns may be fine discontinuous chlorotic streaks between the veins, or irregular blotches or mottles on the leaf surface .
The lesions may become necrotic as the disease progresses.
Infected plants may become stunted in growth with short internodes.
An early-infected plant may die sooner or later without emergence of earhead.
The disease may also affect the grain yield and quality by reducing the size and weight of the kernels.

diseases.name: sorghum mycosphaerella leaf spot
symptoms: The infected leaves show circular to irregular brown to black spots with yellow halos that coalesce to form large necrotic areas. The spots may have grayish centers with dark margins. The infected leaves may curl, dry up, and fall off prematurely .
The infected stems show dark brown to black lesions that girdle the stem and cause wilting and death of the plant.
The infected heads show brown to black spots that enlarge and cover the entire head surface. The heads may become sterile or produce shriveled and discolored seeds .

diseases.name: sorghum northern leaf blight
symptoms: According to one source, sorghum maculopathy is characterized by a convex anterior protrusion (bowing towards the vitreous cavity) of the macula associated with high myopia and a posterior staphyloma. The macula is the central part of the retina that is responsible for sharp vision. A staphyloma is an abnormal bulging of the eye wall. The symptoms of sorghum maculopathy may include blurred or distorted vision, central scotoma (a blind spot in the center of the visual field), metamorphopsia (a distortion of shapes or sizes of objects), and reduced contrast sensitivity.

diseases.name: sorghum sheath blight
symptoms: On seedlings: The disease appears as small narrow elongated spots in the initial stage and in due course they extend along the length of the leaf. The spots are reddish-purple or tan in color and coalesce to form large lesions. The infected leaves may wither or appear pale-green, and the plants may be stunted or die .

On older plants: The typical symptoms are long elliptical necrotic lesions, straw colored in the center with dark margins. The lesions may cover the entire leaf blade or form parallel stripes along the veins. The infected leaves may dry up prematurely, resulting in reduced photosynthesis and grain filling .

diseases.name: sorghum target spot disease
symptoms: The disease initially appears as small, circular, brown to tan spots on the lower leaves of the plant .
The spots enlarge and coalesce, forming irregular-shaped lesions with dark brown margins and gray centers .
The lesions may have concentric rings or a target-like appearance, giving the disease its name .
The infected leaves may turn yellow, dry up, and fall off prematurely, resulting in defoliation and reduced photosynthesis .
The disease can also affect the leaf sheaths, stalks, and panicles of the plant, causing discoloration, rotting, and poor grain filling .

diseases.name: soybean alternaria black spot disease
symptoms: The lesions may be sunken, cracked, or corky, and may girdle the stems or petioles, causing wilting or death of the plant parts .
The lesions may also reduce seed quality and germination.

diseases.name: soybean anthracnose
symptoms: Irregular brown spots or lesions on stems, pods, and petioles . The lesions may be embedded with black fruiting bodies (acervuli) that have tiny spines (setae) that can be seen with a hand lens .
Brown cankers on petioles that may cause defoliation or wilting . The petioles may have a "shepherd's crook" appearance.
Leaf vein reddening and leaf rolling .
Reduced seed number, size, or quality due to pod infection . The seeds may have brown or gray areas with black specks.

diseases.name: soybean bacterial spot
symptoms: Small, circular, water-soaked spots on the leaves, stems, and pods .
Spots enlarge and become raised, reddish-brown pustules with yellow halos .
Pustules may coalesce and form irregular lesions that crack and ooze bacterial exudate .
Infected leaves may turn yellow, brown, or black and fall off prematurely .
Infected pods may shrivel and drop seeds or produce discolored and malformed seeds .

diseases.name: soybean blight
symptoms: Bacterial blight: Small, angular, water-soaked spots that turn yellow and then brown with yellowish-green halos on leaves; spots may merge to form large dead patches; dead tissue may fall out giving a ragged appearance; infection may also occur on stems, petioles, pods, and seeds .

Southern blight: Water-soaked lesions at or below the soil line; lesions may girdle the stem and cause wilting and death; white fungal growth and small brown sclerotia may be seen on the stem base and surrounding soil.

Frogeye leaf spot: Small dark spots that enlarge to circular or irregular lesions with gray-brown centers and reddish-purple borders on leaves; gray fungal growth may be seen on the underside of lesions.

diseases.name: soybean downy mildew
symptoms: Small, light green spots (not water-soaked) on upper leaf surfaces that enlarge and turn pale to bright yellow .
The spots may coalesce into large irregular brown areas with yellow margins .
Portions of the infected area may fall out, giving the leaves a ragged appearance.
Infection of pods and seed can also occur, resulting in a reduction in seed quality and marketability.

diseases.name: soybean gray mold
symptoms: Gray to white lesions at the nodes that expand and girdle the stem .
Wilting, yellowing, and death of the upper leaves and branches .
Infected pods that become bleached and shrunken .
Infected seeds that are cracked, shriveled, and dull .

diseases.name: soybean gray spot
symptoms: Soybean gray spot disease causes circular to irregular leaf lesions that are 1/8-1/4 inch in diameter with gray to brown centers and reddish purple margins. The lesions may coalesce and cause blighting of leaves (Figure 1). Visible gray fungal growth may be seen on the underside of the lesions. The disease may also affect stems, branches and pods, causing black to gray spots, blotches and streaks.

diseases.name: soybean herbicide phytotoxicity
symptoms: Herbicide phytotoxicity in soybeans can cause a variety of symptoms depending on the herbicide used. For example, triazole (DMI) fungicides can cause interveinal chlorosis and necrosis that is commonly associated with either Sudden Death Syndrome or Brown Stem Rot. Another example is the ILeVO seed treatment, which can cause the tips of the cotyledons to turn a yellow-brown color.

diseases.name: soybean leaf blight
symptoms: Bacterial blight: Small, water-soaked spots that turn yellow and then dark brown to black with a yellow border. The spots often coalesce to form irregular brown patches. Portions of the infected area may fall out, giving the leaves a ragged appearance .
Bacterial pustule: Small yellow spots with reddish-brown centers that may develop into raised pustules with yellow halos.
Brown spot: Small dark brown spots that may or may not have a halo. Brown spots may coalesce and infected leaves may turn yellow, creating leaves that appear golden with brown spots or patches.
Downy mildew: Pale green to yellow spots on the upper leaf surface that correspond to grayish-white tufts of fungal growth on the lower leaf surface.

Frogeye leaf spot: Small dark spots that enlarge to circular or irregular leaf lesions with gray-brown centers and darker reddish-purple borders. Visible gray fungal growth may be seen on the underside of the lesions .
Cercospora leaf blight: Reddish-purple to bronze discoloration of the upper leaves that may spread to the entire plant. Infected leaves may curl upward and drop prematurely .

diseases.name: soybean magnesium deficiency
symptoms: The chlorosis starts from the leaf margins and progresses towards the center, leaving a green triangle at the base of the leaf .
As the deficiency worsens, the chlorotic areas turn brown and necrotic, and the leaves may curl and drop prematurely .
Magnesium deficiency can also affect the flowering and pod setting of soybeans, reducing the yield and seed quality .

diseases.name: soybean mosaic disease
symptoms: Mosaic and mottling. These are the most common and characteristic symptoms of SMV infection. They appear as light and dark green patches on individual leaves, especially on young, rapidly growing leaves . The patches may become raised or blistered along the main veins in some cases .

diseases.name: soybean nitrogen deficiency
symptoms: Pale green to yellow leaves, starting from the older leaves at the bottom of the plant and moving upward as the deficiency progresses .
Stunted growth, reduced branching, fewer nodes, and smaller leaf size .
Reduced pod retention and seed fill, leading to fewer seeds per pod, smaller seeds, and lower seed protein and oil content .

diseases.name: soybean phytotoxicity
symptoms: According to a study by Iowa State University, some of the symptoms of soybean injury from herbicides are chlorosis (yellowing), necrosis (browning), spe

diseases.name: soybean sooty blotch
symptoms: According to , Cercospora leaf blight symptoms appear on the upper leaves first and then progress downward. The symptoms include reddish-purple to bronze lesions on the upper leaf surface, which may coalesce and cause premature defoliation (Figure 1). The lower leaf surface may have a silvery-gray appearance due to fungal growth.

According to , purple seed stain symptoms appear on the seed coat as irregular purple blotches or spots, which may cover up to 50% of the seed surface (Figure 2). The seed may also have reduced germination, vigor, and oil content.

diseases.name: soybean sunscald
symptoms: Reddish-brown discoloration of the upper or lower leaf surface, usually along the veins or interveinal tissue .
The discoloration may be translucent when held up to the light and may have a yellow halo around it .
The affected leaf tissue may become sunken, necrotic, or fall out, giving the leaves a ragged appearance .
The symptoms are usually more severe on the upper leaves in the canopy that are exposed to direct sunlight .

diseases.name: soybean target spot disease
symptoms: Reddish-brown lesions, irregularly shaped, with concentric rings and yellow halos, on leaves, petioles, stems, pods, and seeds .
Lesions vary in size and shape depending on the plant part and the stage of infection .
Lesions may coalesce and cause defoliation, premature senescence, pod abortion, and seed discoloration .

diseases.name: soybean virus disease
symptoms: Leaf mottling and mosaic: Virus-infected leaves may show a mosaic of light and dark areas of green or yellow. This symptom is typical for SMV, BPMV, AMV, SVNV, and other viruses . Bumpy, rough leaves (rugose): Some viruses can cause young leaves to have a raised, blistered or distorted appearance. This symptom is common for BPMV and SMV . Seed coat mottling, bleeding hilum, discoloration: Some viruses can affect the seed quality and appearance by causing mottled patterns, dark spots or streaks on the seed coat or hilum (the point where the seed attaches to the pod). This symptom is associated with BPMV, SMV, AMV, TRV, and TSV .

Plant stunting: Some viruses can reduce the plant growth and development by interfering with photosynthesis and other metabolic processes. This symptom is often seen in plants infected with SMV, BPMV, AMV,

diseases.name: spike neck rice blast
symptoms: Lesions on the neck are grayish brown and can cause girdling, making the neck and the panicle fall over.
If infection of the neck occurs before milky stage, no grain is formed, but if infection occurs later, poor quality grains are formed.
Neck and node blast can also cause whiteheads or white panicles, similar to stem borer infection.

diseases.name: sweet potato bacterial wilt
symptoms: The leaves become smaller, narrower, twisted, curled, or distorted in shape.
The veins of the leaves become pale or yellowish, while the interveinal areas remain green or show a mottled pattern.
The plants grow shorter and produce fewer vines than healthy plants.
The tubers develop longitudinal cracks on their surface and show brown or black spots inside their flesh.

diseases.name: sweet potato cercospora leaf spot
symptoms: Leaf spot: Small, circular to irregular spots with dark brown margins and light brown or gray centers appear on the upper surface of the leaves. The spots may coalesce and cause leaf blight or defoliation. Black dots (conidia) may be visible in the center of the spots .
Leaf petiole and stem blight: Dark brown lesions may develop on the leaf petioles and stems, causing wilting and dieback of the vines. The lesions may girdle the stem and cause plant death .

diseases.name: sweet potato frost damage
symptoms: Frost damage can cause various symptoms on sweet potato roots, such as darkening, softening, shriveling, cracking, and rotting . These symptoms can reduce the quality and yield of the crop .

Frost damage can also affect the vines of sweet potatoes, causing them to wilt, blacken, and die . This can reduce the photosynthesis and water uptake of the plant, and expose the roots to cold air temperatures.

diseases.name: sweet potato leaf spot disease
symptoms: Brown leaf spot and stem blight caused by Alternaria bataticola: Small, irregular to circular, dark brown spots on lower leaves, and range in size from a pinpoint to 1/8 inch. The spots may coalesce and cause leaf blight. Dark brown to black lesions may also appear on stems and petioles. Infected tubers may have sunken, dark brown areas .

Cercospora leaf spot caused by Cercospora bataticola: Small, circular to angular, grayish-white spots with dark brown margins on older leaves. The spots may coalesce and cause premature defoliation. No symptoms are observed on tubers.

Viral leaf spot caused by Sweet potato feathery mottle virus: Mottled yellow or green spots on leaves that may become distorted or wrinkled. The spots may be more pronounced along the veins. Infected plants may be stunted and produce fewer or smaller tubers.

diseases.name: sweet potato magnesium deficiency
symptoms: One of the main symptoms of magnesium deficiency is yellowing leaves , especially on the older leaves. The yellowing occurs between the veins, while the veins remain green.

This is because magnesium is used by the plant to make chlorophyll, which gives the leaves their green color. The yellowing may also be accompanied by brown spots or necrosis on the leaf margins or interveinal areas.

diseases.name: sweet potato phytotoxicity
symptoms: They are circular or irregular spots on the leaves that are initially light brown or grayish-white and later become dark brown or black.
The spots may coalesce and cause extensive leaf blight and defoliation. The disease may also affect the stems and petioles, causing them to become brown and brittle .

diseases.name: sweet potato scab disease
symptoms: They are brown to reddish brown, round sunken lesions on the roots that have a solid black center, surrounded by a soft, pinkish ring of decaying tissues.
The lesions become hard, sunken and completely blackened due to the presence of mature mycelium and stromatic tissue.
The disease may also affect the stems and leaves, causing them to become brown and wilted .

diseases.name: sweet potato sooty blotch
symptoms: Small, circular to irregular spots appear on the leaves, usually starting from the older ones. The spots are brown to black with purple margins and may coalesce to form large blighted areas. The spots may also have concentric rings or a target-like appearance .

Dark brown to black lesions appear on the stems, often near the leaf nodes or wounds. The lesions may girdle the stems and cause wilting and dieback of the foliage. The lesions may also produce black conidia under humid conditions .

Dark brown to black decay occurs on the roots, usually starting from the stem end or wounds. The decay may be superficial or deep, depending on the infection stage. The decayed tissue may be dry or moist and may have a bitter taste .

diseases.name: sweet potato virus disease
symptoms: Leaf reduction and deformation: The leaves become smaller, narrower, and distorted in shape .
Vein clearing or mosaic: The veins of the leaves become pale or yellow, and the leaf lamina shows a mottled pattern of green and yellow patches .
Stunting: The plants grow poorly and have reduced biomass and yield .
Chlorosis: The leaves become yellow or pale green, especially along the margins .
Necrosis: The leaves develop brown or black spots or lesions that may coalesce and cause leaf death .

diseases.name: wheat bacterial leaf blight
symptoms: White or grayish-white powdery spots or patches on the leaves, stems, flowers, or fruits .
Yellowing, curling, wilting, or browning of the infected leaves .
Reduced yield, quality, size, and flavor of the pumpkins .
Bud drop and stunted vine growth in severe cases.

diseases.name: wheat crustose leaf blight
symptoms: Anorexia and lethargy: infected amphibians may lose appetite and become less active .

Skin abnormalities: infected amphibians may experience excessive shedding of skin, which appears opaque and gray-white or tan in color. They may also develop red skin, convulsions, lack of righting reflex (a reflex that corrects the orientation of the body after it has been taken out of its normal upright position), abnormal feeding behavior, and discoloration near the mouth .
Mortality: infected amphibians may die from cardiac arrest or electrolyte imbalance caused by impaired osmoregulation and respiration through their water-permeable skin .

diseases.name: wheat leaf blight
symptoms: Water-soaked spots on leaves that expand and turn from gray-green to tan-white .
Irregular streaks or blotches on leaves that may coalesce and kill the entire leaf .
Slimy droplets on leaves in wet weather that are bacterial exudates.

diseases.name: wheat mosaic disease
symptoms: Stunting of plants and reduced tillering
Light green to yellow mottled mosaic on leaves
Yellow streaks running parallel to the veins on leaves
Tightly rolled and trapped leaves due to mite feeding damage
Reduced grain yield and quality

diseases.name: wheat powdery mildew
symptoms: White to gray cottony fungal growth on the upper leaf surface, stems, and heads .
Yellowing, later turning tan or brown, of the leaf tissue on the opposite side of the leaf from the white mold growth.
Reduced vigor, heading, and grain filling of colonized plants.
Premature death of heavily infected leaves.

diseases.name: wheat septoria blotch
symptoms: Small chlorotic spots on the leaves that appear soon after seedlings emerge in the fall or spring.
Light tan lesions that enlarge and become long, narrow and delimited by leaf veins or irregularly or elliptically shaped.
Necrotic lesions on leaves and stems that develop after infected cells collapse.

diseases.name: wheat spindle streak mosaic disease
symptoms: Stunting of infected plants, which may be splayed on the ground and do not tiller as well as healthy plants. Foliar symptoms such as a light green to yellow mottled mosaic; more advanced infections may develop yellow streaks running parallel to the veins. Symptoms are more pronounced when temperatures are above 70℃and less visible when temperatures are below 70℃. Tightly rolled and trapped leaves that result from feeding damage by large numbers of wheat curl mites. Sterile wheat heads that result from severe infections that reduce yield potential and quality.

diseases.name: wheat spot blight
symptoms: Bleaching of florets in the spike or head, which may extend to the entire spike in severe cases .
Tan to brown discoloration at the base of the head .
Shriveled, light weight, and chalky white or pink kernels, resembling tombstones .
Reduced grain yield and quality, affecting photosynthesis, grain filling, and seed germination .

diseases.name: wheat stripe leaf blight
symptoms: Water-soaked streaks on the leaves that are irregular and translucent, and may have bacterial ooze within them . Yellowing and browning of the leaf tissue as the streaks become necrotic and reduce the photosynthetic capacity of the plant .
Stunting and reduced tillering of the infected plants due to impaired growth and development .

diseases.name: wheat stripe mosaic disease
symptoms: Yellowed mosaic pattern of parallel discontinuous streaks on leaves of infected plants . Mottled yellow leaves as symptoms progress . Stunted, discolored and rosetted plants infected before early tillering. The extent of stunting and rosetting in a field gives some indication as to the severity of the disease and ultimate yield loss .

Rolled and trapped leaves due to mite feeding damage. On a young plant, the tip of a new leaf often is trapped in the rolled leaf immediately below it so that the developing leaf is curled back on itself, forming a loop .

diseases.name: wheat yellow leaf spot blight
symptoms: Yellowish or chlorotic flecks on leaves, especially those in contact with the soil
Irregular lesions, brown-to-reddish brown in color, with somewhat bleached centers and bright yellow margins
Small, dark brown to black specks ( pycnidia ) within the lesions
The premature death of leaves and reduced grain filling

diseases.name: wheat yellow stripe rust
symptoms: Yellow streaks on the leaves, leaf sheaths, glumes and awns, followed by small, bright yellow, elongated uredial pustules arranged in conspicuous rows (Figure 1).
Long, narrow yellow stripes on the leaves in some varieties.
Brown and dry tissues as the plant matures or becomes stressed.
Plant stunting in case of severe early infection.

diseases.name: tobacco brown leaf spot
symptoms: This disease are pain, redness, and rash on the skin, usually with a well-demarcated raised border.
The rash may be bright red, firm, swollen, blistered, or necrotic.
The infection may also cause fever, chills, shivering, fatigue, headache, vomiting, and malaise .
